PCR Master Mix
PK Pharmacokinetic Parameters
Enter IV and/or PO plasma concentration data to calculate key PK parameters. All AUC values calculated by the trapezoidal method.
💧 Pip Tip: AUC extrapolated to ∞ using AUClast + Clast/kel. Terminal kel fitted by linear regression on the log-linear terminal phase (last 3+ points). CL = DoseIV/AUCIV,∞. Vd = CL/kel. F% = (AUCPO/DosePO) / (AUCIV/DoseIV) × 100.
pH Buffer / Henderson-Hasselbalch
Common Buffer Reference
BufferpKaUseful pH rangeNotes
Acetate4.753.8 – 5.8Cell-compatible, volatile
MES6.155.5 – 6.7Good ionic strength control
Phosphate (pKa2)7.206.3 – 8.0Most common, precipitates Ca²⁺/Mg²⁺
HEPES7.486.8 – 8.2Cell culture gold standard
Tris8.067.2 – 9.0Temp-sensitive; −0.028/°C
Glycine9.788.6 – 10.6SDS-PAGE running buffer
💧 Pip Tip: Tris pKa shifts −0.028 per °C. At 37°C: pKa ≈ 7.7. Always measure pH at working temperature.
Molecular Biology
Gibson · Restriction cloning · Transformation · Miniprep
Protocol Cloning · Transformation
Prep DNA purification · Cleanup
Gibson Assembly (NEB HiFi E5510) 27 Jan 2025 ▶
NEB Gibson Assembly Protocol (E5510)
Overview — The 3-Step Workflow
Gibson Assembly joins linearised DNA fragments that share overlapping sequences at their ends. The HiFi Master Mix contains an exonuclease (chews back 5′ ends to expose overlaps), a polymerase (fills gaps), and a ligase (seals nicks). The workflow is: (1) PCR-amplify vector and insert with primers that introduce the overlapping sequences → (2) DpnI digest to remove template plasmid → (3) assemble the purified fragments.
1 — Design Overlapping Primers (before PCR)
  1. Overlaps of 15–30 bp are optimal for NEBHiFi. Design your primers so that the 3′ end anneals to your template (binding region, Tm ~60°C) and the 5′ overhang carries the overlap sequence matching the adjacent fragment.
  2. Check that overlap regions do not form strong secondary structure (Mfold) and avoid overlaps that fall on repetitive sequence.
  3. The vector is typically amplified by inverse PCR (back-to-back primers) to linearise it and add overhangs. The insert is amplified from its template with forward/reverse primers carrying the vector-matching overhangs.
Overlaps longer than 80 bp can reduce Gibson efficiency. Keep them 20–30 bp unless you have a specific reason for longer arms.
2 — PCR → DpnI → Cleanup (one continuous workflow)
  1. Amplify vector backbone and insert separately using Q5 Hi-Fi polymerase (see calculator below)
  2. Without purifying, add DpnI directly to the PCR tube. DpnI is active in Q5 Master Mix buffer — no buffer exchange needed. See DpnI calculator below for volumes. Incubate 37°C × 60 min.
  3. Purify with NucleoSpin column. Elute in 15–20 uL. Measure by NanoDrop — needed for the Gibson calculator.
  4. Optional gel check: run 2–3 uL of the purified product on a 1% gel to confirm band size and absence of non-specific products. Only strictly necessary if NanoDrop yield is unexpectedly low or if you want to rule out contaminating bands before assembly.
4 — Gibson Assembly Reaction
  1. Set up on ice. Add DNA fragments and water first; add NEBHiFi 2× Master Mix last.
  2. pmols = (ng × 1000) / (bp × 650) — aim for 0.06 pmol vector, 2× molar excess of each insert
  3. Incubate at 50°C: 15 min (2–3 fragments) or 60 min (4–6 fragments)
  4. Store on ice or at −20°C. Transform 2 uL into NEB Stable competent cells.
Pip Tip
If you get very few colonies but positive controls work, the most common causes are: (1) incomplete DpnI digestion (too little enzyme or short incubation) letting template through, (2) re-circularisation of uncut vector — confirm linearisation by gel before assembly, (3) poor overlap design — check Tm of overlap and absence of secondary structure. White/blue colony screening is not applicable here; always verify by colony PCR and Sanger sequencing.
Step 1 — Pre-Gibson PCR (linearise vector & insert with overhangs)
Vector (template plasmid → linearised backbone)
Insert (template → PCR fragment with overhangs)
Step 2 — DpnI Digestion (remove template plasmid)
Why: Q5 PCR amplifies both your product AND the original template plasmid. DpnI cuts methylated DNA (the bacterial template), leaving only your unmethylated PCR product.
Add directly to the PCR tube after PCR — no purification needed before DpnI.
ComponentVolume
PCR product (from 50 uL rxn)50 uL
DpnI (NEB)1 uL
10× rCutSmart Buffer5.5 uL
  1. Incubate at 37°C × 60 min
  2. Store at 4°C or proceed directly to PCR cleanup (NucleoSpin)
  3. After cleanup, measure concentration by NanoDrop — use this value in the Gibson Assembly Calculator below
Step 3 — Gibson Assembly Calculator
Based on: NEB E5510 protocol — NEBHiFi 2× Master Mix, 10 uL total reaction, 0.06 pmol vector target, 2:1 insert:vector molar ratio
Vector (post-PCR + DpnI + cleanup)
Settings
Export protocol steps
Restriction Enzyme Cloning (NEB) 12 Feb 2025 ▶
What is restriction cloning? Restriction enzymes cut DNA at specific short sequences (recognition sites), leaving either blunt or sticky (overhanging) ends. Two compatible sticky ends can be ligated directionally. The workflow is: (1) digest both vector and insert with the same enzyme(s) → (2) optionally dephosphorylate vector → (3) gel-purify or column-clean → (4) ligate → (5) transform. Use NEBcloner to select compatible enzymes and buffers.
1 — Choose Your Enzymes
  1. Use NEBcloner to identify restriction sites in your vector and insert, and to check buffer compatibility if doing a double digest
  2. Single digest: simple but can allow insert in both orientations — use if the insert is directional by sequence context, or if you will screen by colony PCR
  3. Double digest: two different enzymes giving incompatible ends force directional cloning — preferred when possible. Confirm both enzymes are active in the same buffer (NEBcloner shows this). Most NEB enzymes are compatible with CutSmart or rCutSmart.
  4. Check for internal sites: confirm neither enzyme cuts within your insert or within the region you want to keep in the vector
For double digests, confirm both enzymes have ≥75% activity in the chosen buffer. If not, do sequential digests with a column cleanup in between.
2 — Digest Reaction
Standard: 1 ug DNA in 50 uL. Scale down to 20 uL for small amounts. Always add enzyme last, keep on ice.
Digest Setup Calculator
NEB standard digest — 10 U/ug DNA, 1× CutSmart buffer, 37°C × 60 min (or as recommended for your enzyme)
Enzymes (up to 2 for double digest)
  1. Assemble on ice in order: water → buffer → DNA → enzyme(s) last
  2. Mix gently by pipetting (do NOT vortex); spin briefly
  3. Incubate at the enzyme-specific temperature (most NEB enzymes: 37°C) for 60 min. Time-Saver enzymes (marked TS): 15 min is sufficient.
  4. Stop reaction: heat-inactivate at 65°C × 20 min (most HF enzymes) or 80°C × 20 min — check the NEB datasheet for your specific enzyme. Alternatively add loading dye and proceed directly to gel.
Not all enzymes are heat-inactivatable. Check the NEB datasheet — enzymes not HI (e.g. SmaI, NotI) must be removed by column cleanup before ligation.
3 — Gel Verification and Purification
  1. Run the entire digest on a 0.8–1% TAE agarose gel with a 1 kb ladder
  2. Confirm bands at expected sizes. Gel-extract both vector and insert — this removes uncut and partially-cut DNA, which is a major source of background colonies. Use NucleoSpin Gel & PCR Clean-up. Elute in 15–20 uL. Measure by NanoDrop.
  3. Minimise UV exposure when excising bands — use long-wave UV (365 nm) or a blue-light transilluminator if available
4 — Dephosphorylation of Vector (recommended for single digests)
  1. Why: After a single digest, the linearised vector can re-ligate on itself (giving background colonies with no insert). Removing the 5′ phosphate groups with a phosphatase prevents re-ligation — only the insert (which retains its 5′ phosphates) can close the circle.
  2. Add 1 uL Quick CIP (NEB) or rSAP directly to the digested, gel-purified vector; incubate 37°C × 10 min
  3. Heat-inactivate: 80°C × 2 min (Quick CIP) or 65°C × 5 min (rSAP)
  4. Proceed directly to ligation — no additional cleanup needed after CIP/SAP inactivation
For double digests with incompatible ends, dephosphorylation is less critical but still recommended if background is a concern.
5 — Ligation
Molar ratio: 3:1 to 5:1 insert:vector (molar) is standard. More insert = more efficient ligation, but too much can inhibit. The calculator below computes volumes based on your NanoDrop concentrations.
Ligation Calculator
T4 DNA Ligase (NEB M0202) — 20 uL reaction, 400 U T4 ligase, 16°C × 16 h or RT × 10 min (Quick Ligation)
Vector
Insert
  1. Assemble on ice: water → 10× T4 Ligase Buffer (thaw and vortex to dissolve ATP) → vector → insert → T4 DNA Ligase last
  2. Standard ligation: 16°C × 16 h (overnight in cold room or PCR block). Good for difficult ligations (blunt ends, long fragments).
  3. Quick Ligation (NEB M2200): 25°C × 5–15 min. Convenient for routine sticky-end ligations. Less efficient for blunt or single-cut.
  4. Heat-inactivate at 65°C × 10 min, then chill on ice
  5. Transform 2–5 uL into competent cells (see Transformation protocol)
T4 Ligase Buffer contains ATP which degrades on freeze-thaw. Use fresh aliquots; avoid more than 3 freeze-thaw cycles. Old buffer is a common cause of failed ligations.
Export protocol steps
Transformation of Competent Cells (DH5α / NEB Stable) — ▶
What is this? Chemical transformation introduces plasmid DNA into competent E. coli by briefly permeabilising the cell membrane with heat shock. Used to amplify plasmid DNA after cloning (Gibson, restriction ligation) or to propagate existing constructs. Recovery in rich media (SOC) before plating allows cells to express the antibiotic resistance gene before selection. Best done in the evening so plates incubate overnight.
Best done last thing in the afternoon/evening
Preparation
  1. Thaw competent E. coli on ice — DH5α: ~20–30 min; NEB Stable: 5–10 min
  2. Pre-warm LB agar plates with appropriate antibiotic at 37°C
Transformation
  1. Mix 1–5 uL plasmid DNA (10 pg–100 ng) with 25–50 uL competent cells; flick gently to mix
  2. Incubate on ice 2 min
  3. Heat shock: 42°C × 30–60 s (typically 45 s; 30 s for NEB Stable)
  4. Back on ice 2 min
  5. Add 450 uL LB/SOC; incubate 37°C × 1 h at 200–250 rpm (30°C for lentiviral plasmids)
Plating
  1. Spin briefly; resuspend in 50 uL LB/SOC; spread all on pre-warmed selective plates
  2. Incubate overnight 37°C (30°C for lentiviral)
Colony Selection
  1. Pick single colonies into 5 mL LB + antibiotic (15 mL tube); incubate overnight 37°C, 150–200 rpm
Miniprep — GeneJET (Thermo K0502/K0503) 06 Feb 2025 ▶
What is this? A miniprep isolates plasmid DNA from a small overnight E. coli culture (5 mL) using alkaline lysis followed by silica-column purification. The GeneJET kit is reliable for routine plasmid prep — typical yield 5–20 ug, elution in 50 uL. Use a midiprep or maxiprep for larger amounts (transfection-grade DNA, virus production).
GeneJET Miniprep Kit (Thermo) — All steps at RT; centrifuge ≥12,000 × g. Culture 5 mL overnight in LB + antibiotic.
1 — Harvest & Lyse
  1. Centrifuge 5 mL culture at 8000 rpm (6800 × g) × 2 min; discard supernatant
  2. Add 250 uL Resuspension Solution — vortex until pellet is fully resuspended
  3. Add 250 uL Lysis Solution — invert gently 4–6× until clear; do not vortex
  4. Add 350 uL Neutralization Solution — invert 4–6×; centrifuge 5 min to pellet debris
2 — Bind, Wash & Elute
  1. Transfer supernatant to GeneJET Spin Column in collection tube; centrifuge 1 min; discard flow-through
  2. Add 500 uL Wash Solution; centrifuge 30–60 s; discard flow-through. Repeat wash once.
  3. Centrifuge empty column 1 min to dry the membrane
  4. Transfer column to a clean 1.5 mL tube; add 50 uL Elution Buffer (or nuclease-free H₂O, pre-warmed to 65°C for higher yield); incubate 2 min at RT; centrifuge 2 min
Do not over-lyse — blue lysate turning clear is the correct endpoint. Over-inversion can shear chromosomal DNA into the eluate, reducing purity (260/230 ratio).
NucleoSpin Gel & PCR Clean-up (Macherey-Nagel) 28 Jan 2025 ▶
What is this? A silica-membrane spin column that removes salts, enzymes, primers, and buffer components from PCR reactions or gel slices, recovering purified DNA. Use the PCR cleanup mode for clean PCR products (e.g. after DpnI digest). Use the gel extraction mode when you need to isolate a specific band away from other fragments or uncut vector.
NucleoSpin Kit Manual (MN) — Two modes: PCR cleanup or gel extraction (see below). All spins at 11,000 × g.
PCR Cleanup Mode
  1. Add sample directly to column in collection tube; spin 30 s; discard flow-through
  2. Wash: 700 uL NT3; spin 30 s; discard. Repeat once.
  3. Dry: spin 1 min (empty column)
  4. Elute: add 15–20 uL NE buffer; wait 1 min RT; transfer to fresh tube; spin 1 min
Gel Extraction Mode
  1. Excise band under long-wave UV (365 nm) or blue-light transilluminator — minimise exposure time to reduce DNA damage
  2. Weigh gel slice; add 200 uL NTI buffer per 100 mg gel; incubate 50°C until dissolved (5–10 min, vortex occasionally)
  3. Transfer to column; spin 30 s; discard flow-through
  4. Wash: 700 uL NT3; spin 30 s; discard. Repeat once. Dry: spin 1 min.
  5. Elute: add 15–20 uL NE buffer (pre-warm to 70°C for fragments <500 bp); wait 1 min; spin 1 min
Gel extraction yield is lower than PCR cleanup (~70–80%). Measure by NanoDrop after elution — do not assume concentration.
CRISPR
gRNA design · KO (PX458) · Knock-in (HDR) · Electroporation · Single-cell sorting
Reference Guide design · Off-target scoring · Donor strategy
Protocol Editing · Delivery · Cell enrichment
gRNA & Donor Design 21 Feb 2025 ▶
What is this? CRISPR-Cas9 edits genomic DNA by making a double-strand break at a locus defined by a guide RNA (gRNA). For knock-outs (KO), error-prone NHEJ repair introduces frameshift indels that disrupt the open reading frame. For knock-ins (KI), a short single-stranded donor (ssODN) is co-delivered so that HDR introduces a precise modification — a tag, point mutation, or short sequence. Careful gRNA and donor design is the most important determinant of success.
Before you start: Write down your "source of truth" — which transcript/isoform you are targeting, the genomic coordinates of the edit site, and which protein termini is being modified. This avoids ambiguity later when interpreting sequencing data or troubleshooting.
1 — Retrieve Gene Sequence (NCBI)
  1. Go to NCBI Gene and search for the gene of interest (e.g. "RBM39 human")
  2. Navigate to Genomic regions, transcripts, and products
  3. Click GenBank (top right) → Send to → File. Download the RefSeq entry (NM_XXXXX).
  4. If unsure which is the canonical isoform, check UniProt for the canonical sequence annotation
  5. Open the GenBank file in SnapGene: untick all features → scroll to the first (or full-length) protein → mark the CDS region
2 — Select Region of Interest
  1. In SnapGene, navigate to your intended edit site (e.g. stop codon for C-terminal tag, start codon for N-terminal tag)
  2. Select a ~200 bp region centred on the edit site — this will be your gRNA design template and donor context
  3. For N-terminal tags: target close to the start codon. For C-terminal tags: target just before the stop codon. Aim to cut as close to the insertion site as possible to maximise HDR efficiency.
3 — Design gRNA Candidates (IDT)
  1. Go to IDT CRISPR Cas9 Design Tool — paste your ~200 bp region
  2. IDT will return ranked gRNA candidates with on-target and off-target scores. Shortlist 2–3 candidates.
  3. (Knock-in only) For each candidate, verify in SnapGene that silencing the PAM site (to prevent re-cutting after HDR) does not change the amino acid sequence — use synonymous substitutions whenever possible
  4. (Knock-out) Target a constitutive exon shared across all relevant isoforms. Early exon frameshift indels are most reliable. Avoid exons subject to alternative splicing.
Always shortlist multiple gRNAs and test at least 2 in parallel. Off-target score alone does not predict editing efficiency — on-target score and proximity to edit site both matter.
Pip Tip
Evaluating off-target sites — seed region rule: When reviewing off-target predictions from IDT or CRISPOR, pay attention to where the mismatches fall. Cas9 is highly intolerant to mismatches in the seed region (~10 nt immediately upstream of the PAM) — a single mismatch there is usually sufficient to abolish cleavage at that site. Mismatches in the PAM-distal end (positions 11–20 from PAM) are much better tolerated and should not be relied upon for specificity. So if you cannot find a gRNA with zero predicted off-targets: prioritise candidates where all predicted off-target sites carry ≥1 mismatch within positions 1–10 upstream of the PAM. Those off-targets are effectively inert.
4 — (Knock-in) Design the Donor Template (ssODN)
  1. Start from the WT genomic sequence around the cut site. In SnapGene, introduce your modification (tag sequence, point mutation, etc.).
  2. PAM silencing: mutate the PAM (NGG → NGA or similar) or the seed region of the protospacer (the ~12 bp proximal to PAM) to prevent Cas9 re-cutting the edited allele. Synonymous codon changes are preferred.
  3. Keep the donor ≤200 bp total. Place your modification as close to the cut site as possible — HDR efficiency drops with distance from the DSB.
  4. Typical design: ~50–80 bp homology arm — [tag/mutation] — ~50–80 bp homology arm
  5. Order as a standard desalted ssODN from IDT (Ultramer if >60 nt, standard PAGE-purified for shorter)
5 — Pre-plan Controls and Record-Keeping
  1. Plan controls before starting wet-lab work: untreated parental cells · mock electroporation (no RNP) · non-targeting guide
  2. Record guide IDs, donor version IDs, transcript choice, and reagent lot numbers before starting
  3. After the experiment: MiSeq amplicon sequencing is the definitive readout of editing outcome — plan your genotyping primers now (flanking the edit site, amplicon ~200–300 bp)
Pip Tip
Pip Tip — ssODN vs dsDNA donor: ssODNs (<200 nt) are efficient for small knock-ins (tags, point mutations) and are the standard choice for HiBiT or small epitope tag insertion. For larger inserts (>200 bp, e.g. full fluorescent protein), switch to a dsDNA donor (PCR product with homology arms or a plasmid). ssODN asymmetric design (longer arm on the non-template strand relative to the nick) can improve HDR efficiency with some Cas9 variants.
CRISPR Knock-in Apr 2026 ▶
What is this? Targeted insertion of a sequence (tag, fluorescent protein, selection cassette or point mutation) at a genomic locus — CRISPR-Cas9 cuts the locus, HDR incorporates a donor template. Two delivery routes are in use in the lab; pick based on cell line and insert size.
Delivery:
1 — gRNA design (common)
  1. Follow gRNA & Donor Design to shortlist 2–3 gRNAs with a PAM-silencing strategy already worked out.
  2. Cut site should be ≤10 bp from the insertion site — HDR efficiency drops sharply with distance.
2 — Plasmid donor design
  1. Homology arms: 300–600 bp each flanking the insert. Use genomic sequence as template — match your cell line's genotype where relevant.
  2. PAM silencing: mutate the PAM (or seed region) in the donor so Cas9 does not re-cut the repaired allele. Synonymous codon changes preferred.
  3. Clone the donor into a small backbone (pUC19 or equivalent — no eukaryotic promoter needed for HDR). Sanger-verify the entire insert + both junctions before use.
Pip Tip
Optional: Double-cut donor (Zhang et al. 2017, Genome Biol) — Flank the HA–insert–HA cassette with the same sgRNA target + PAM as in the genome. Cas9 then linearises the donor and cuts the locus in the same cell, reported to boost HDR 2–5× in iPSCs and K562. Worth trying in difficult lines or for large inserts. Layout: [sgRNA+PAM] — [5′ HA] — [insert] — [3′ HA] — [sgRNA+PAM].
2 — ssODN donor design
  1. Donor length: ≤200 nt total. Typical layout: ~50–80 bp HA — [insert/mutation] — ~50–80 bp HA.
  2. PAM silencing: mutate PAM (NGG → NGA) or seed region with synonymous substitutions.
  3. Order as IDT Ultramer (>60 nt) or standard PAGE-purified ssODN. Asymmetric designs (longer non-target-strand arm) can improve HDR with some Cas9 variants.
  4. Insert >200 bp? Switch to the plasmid-donor branch above — ssODN is length-limited.
3 — Delivery: PX458 + plasmid donor co-transfection
  1. Clone the gRNA spacer into PX458 via BbsI digest + NEBuilder HiFi assembly. Full step-by-step protocol with volume calculators: PX458 + Plasmid Donor protocol.
  2. Day 0: seed 1 × 10⁶ cells per well in a 6-well plate.
  3. Day 1 transfection (FugeneHD, 6-well): co-deliver 1 ug PX458-gRNA + 1 ug donor plasmid. Starting ratio 1:1; optimise up to 1:3 sgRNA:donor for difficult inserts. 3 uL FugeneHD in 200 uL Opti-MEM total.
  4. Incubate 24–48 h; proceed to sorting before Cas9 expression plateaus.
Plasmid donors can integrate randomly (off-target insertion) — always validate KI by both 5′ and 3′ junction PCR + amplicon sequencing, not just expression of the tag.
3 — Delivery: RNP electroporation + ssODN
  1. Pre-complex Cas9 protein + sgRNA (1:1.2 molar ratio) in nuclease-free buffer, RT 10 min.
  2. Mix RNP with ssODN donor (1–3 uM final in the electroporation tip) immediately before pulse.
  3. Electroporate with Neon (10 uL tip, cell-line-specific programme) or 4D-Nucleofector (cell-line-specific buffer/programme).
  4. Plate into pre-warmed antibiotic-free media for 24 h; swap to standard media thereafter.
See also Electroporation (RNP) for cell-line-specific programmes and buffer recipes.
4 — Enrichment: FACS sort GFP⁺
  1. 8–24 h post-transfection: harvest, stain for viability if desired, sort GFP⁺ (Cas9-expressing) cells.
  2. Deposit single GFP⁺ cells into a 96-well plate for clonal expansion — see Single-Cell Sorting.
4 — Enrichment: recovery + single-cell sort
  1. RNP has no intrinsic selection marker — allow 48–72 h recovery, then bulk-sort live cells into 96-well plates for clonal expansion.
  2. See Single-Cell Sorting.
5 — Genotyping & validation (common)
  1. Design flanking primers (~200–400 bp amplicon) spanning the edit site. For plasmid donors also design junction primers — one anchor outside the HA (in genome), one inside the insert — this distinguishes true KI from random plasmid integration.
  2. Quick screen: Sanger + ICE. Publication-quality: MiSeq amplicon sequencing.
  3. For tagged proteins: confirm by expression readout (HiBiT/fluorescence) and junction PCR — do not trust expression alone.
Pip Tip
Pip Tip — when to use which method:
• RNP + ssODN — small edits (tags ≤200 nt, point mutations), hard-to-transfect lines, primary cells. Faster, no cloning, lowest off-target exposure (transient Cas9).
• PX458 + plasmid donor — larger inserts (HiBiT-mNG fusions, selection cassettes, full FPs), adherent lines that tolerate lipofection, when GFP⁺ enrichment is useful. Longer Cas9 expression → slightly higher off-target risk; always validate by junction PCR.
• Double-cut donor — worth a shot in difficult lines (iPSCs, K562) or when HDR yield from a standard plasmid donor is <1%.
CRISPR KO via PX458 Transfection Mar 2026 ▶
Delivery:
What is this? A two-step CRISPR knock-out workflow using the PX458 plasmid (pSpCas9(BB)-2A-GFP, Addgene #48138), which co-expresses SpCas9 and GFP. The gRNA is cloned directly into PX458 via a ssOligo + NEB HiFi Assembly strategy (no annealing/phosphorylation of separate oligos needed). Cells are then transfected with FugeneHD and GFP-positive cells sorted by FACS 8–24 h later. No donor template — indel-based KO only.
Part 1 — Cloning the gRNA into PX458
Step 1ssOligo Design & Order (IDT)
Order from IDT as Ultramer DNA Oligo (recommended for sequences >60 nt) — IDT Custom Oligos
  1. Design your 20 bp gRNA spacer targeting the gene of interest. Verify PAM (NGG, immediately 3′ of spacer) and assess on-target/off-target scores using Benchling or CRISPOR.
  2. Design the ssOligo insert (≈ 70 nt total):
    • 5′ homology arm (25 bp) — matches PX458 upstream of the BbsI cut site
    • 20 bp gRNA spacer
    • 3′ homology arm (25 bp) — matches PX458 downstream of the BbsI cut site
  3. Order at 100 nmol scale (standard desalt purification is sufficient)
  4. Upon receipt, resuspend in TE buffer + 50 mM NaCl to 100 uM stock; aliquot and freeze at −20°C
  5. Prepare a 1 uM working stock: dilute 1 uL of 100 uM stock into 99 uL buffer
Order the oligo first — you need it before you can assemble. Typical IDT turnaround is 1–3 business days for Ultramers.
Step 2Plasmid Linearisation (BbsI digest)
  1. Digest 1 ug PX458 with BbsI (NEB) at 37°C for 30 min following the manufacturer's protocol — see calculator below
  2. Confirm linearisation on a 1% agarose gel (expected band ≈ 9.3 kb)
  3. Excise the band and purify the linearised plasmid (NucleoSpin or equivalent). Measure concentration by NanoDrop — needed for the calculator below
BbsI Digest Calculator — PX458 Linearisation (20 uL reaction)
NEB BbsI (R0593): 37°C × 30 min in CutSmart Buffer | Target: 1 µg DNA in 20 µL total
Step 3HiFi Assembly & Transformation
NEBuilder HiFi DNA Assembly Master Mix (NEB E2621)
HiFi Assembly Calculator — PX458 + ssOligo (20 uL reaction)
Formula: pmol/uL = (ng/uL × 1000) / (bp × 660) | ssOligo insert:vector ratio = 200:1
Export protocol steps
  1. Set up the reaction on ice using the volumes from the calculator. Add components in order: water → vector → ssOligo → master mix
  2. Incubate at 50°C for 60 min in a thermocycler (lid off or set to 55°C)
  3. Transform 2 uL of assembled product into NEB Stable Competent E. coli (C3040H) — follow the Transformation protocol; note heat shock 42°C × 30 s
  4. Plate on LB+ampicillin (100 ug/mL); pick 6–8 colonies; miniprep and verify by Sanger sequencing across the cloning site
Part 2 — Transfection into Cells
Day 0Cell Seeding
  1. Plate 1 × 10⁶ wild-type cells per well in a 6-well plate
  2. Always include a negative control well (no transfection) seeded in parallel — used to set the GFP gate at FACS and confirm baseline
Day 1Transfection (FugeneHD · 6-well plate)
  1. Tube A — add 1 ug PX458-gRNA plasmid to 100 uL Opti-MEM. Mix gently.
  2. Tube B — add 3 uL FugeneHD (Promega) to 100 uL Opti-MEM. Mix gently.
  3. Combine Tube A + Tube B, mix gently, and incubate 5 min at RT.
  4. Add the entire complex dropwise to the well (cells are already in media — no media change needed).
  5. Incubate cells with the transfection reagent for 8–24 h
  6. Sort GFP-positive cells by FACS to enrich for Cas9-expressing transfected cells → proceed to single-cell sorting for clonal expansion (see Single Cell Sorting protocol)
PX458 expresses GFP as a transfection marker — GFP+ cells are Cas9-expressing but not necessarily edited. Sort into wells for clonal expansion, then genotype by Sanger/ICE analysis to confirm KO.
Pip Tip
PX458-based transfection suits adherent cell lines that are difficult to electroporate. Key differences vs RNP electroporation: (1) longer Cas9 expression window → slightly higher off-target risk; (2) GFP selection is a positive marker for Cas9 delivery, unlike RNP which has no selection; (3) no need to pre-assemble RNP — simpler handling. For hard-to-transfect lines or primary cells, RNP electroporation remains the gold standard.
Electroporation (CRISPR RNP) 23 Oct 2024 ▶
Delivery:
What is this? Electroporation delivers the CRISPR RNP (ribonucleoprotein — Cas9 protein pre-complexed with the annealed gRNA duplex) directly into cells using a brief electrical pulse. RNP delivery avoids DNA integration of Cas9, reduces off-target activity, and gives transient expression that disappears within days. The Neon NxT system uses voltage/width/pulse parameters optimised per cell line — use your validated programme.
Reconstitution — TracrRNA & crRNA
  1. tracrRNA: 20 nmol → 200 uL Nuclease-Free duplex buffer (IDT) → 100 uM stock
  2. crRNA: 2 nmol → 20 uL duplex buffer → 100 uM stock
  3. Donor DNA (knock-in): 2 nmol → 20 uL duplex buffer → 100 uM stock
  4. Spin down after reconstitution
Reconstitution — Cas9
  1. Add 50 uL reconstitution solution to lyophilized S. pyogenes Cas9 → 1 ug/uL
  2. Vortex gently, spin down, incubate 10 min on ice or overnight in fridge
Anneal TracrRNA + crRNA
  1. Add 5 uL of each (TracrRNA + crRNA) per tube (1 tube per crRNA)
  2. Incubate at 95°C × 5 min, then spin down
RNP Complex Formation (per 2 electroporations, ~0.4M cells)
KO: 12 uL R buffer + 1 uL gRNA + 1.2 uL Cas9
KI: add 12 uL R buffer + 1 uL 100 uM Donor
  1. Mix both components and incubate 20 min at RT
Cell Preparation
  1. Trypsinize, neutralize, wash with PBS, resuspend in 1 mL PBS
  2. Count: add 2×10⁵ cells/electroporation to Eppendorf
  3. Remove PBS by centrifugation
  4. Add 2 mL media to 6-well plate; seed same number of cells in negative control well
Electroporation
  1. Fill 50 mL falcon with ethanol, insert tips; aspirate and dry on paper; wash with PBS
  2. Add RNP mix to cell pellet; resuspend gently without generating bubbles
  3. Add 3 mL electrolyte solution to electroporation vessel
  4. Take 10 uL of mix with electroporating pipette → place in vessel → electroporate → dispense into well. Repeat per well.
  5. Leave cells 48–72 h before analysis/sorting
After Electroporation — Pool Validation (Before Single-Cell Sorting)
Why validate the pool first? Single-cell sorting is time-consuming. Confirming editing in the pool first avoids committing to clonal expansion if the edit rate is too low or if the edit is not the right type.
  1. Maintain edited cells as a pooled population for 48–72 h minimum before analysis (allow NHEJ/HDR to resolve)
  2. KO pool: check by MiSeq amplicon sequencing around the cut site — aim for >70% frameshift indels before sorting. Optionally confirm loss of protein by western blot.
  3. KI HiBiT pool: use HiBiT luminescence as a fast functional readout to confirm signal above background. Treat MiSeq as the definitive confirmation of correct on-target integration — luminescence alone can come from partial integrations.
  4. Define a go/no-go criterion before sorting (e.g. >30% HiBiT-positive cells by luminescence or >20% correct-size band by PCR). If below threshold, consider re-electroporating with a different guide.
Pip Tip
For KI experiments, a quick PCR with one primer inside the tag and one outside the homology arm is a fast way to confirm integration before committing to MiSeq. A band = integration (though not necessarily correct). TIDE analysis (tide.nki.nl) on Sanger sequencing of the pool gives a good estimate of editing efficiency and indel spectrum for KO guides.
Knock-in — PX458 + Plasmid Donor Apr 2026 ▶
Strategy: PX458 (pSpCas9(BB)-2A-GFP, Addgene #48138) co-expresses Cas9 and GFP from a single plasmid. Co-transfect with a plasmid donor bearing the insert flanked by homology arms (HAs). GFP⁺ cells sorted by FACS are enriched for Cas9-expressing (and therefore editing-competent) cells. Best for large inserts >200 bp, fluorescent tags, or selection cassettes. The Zhang 2017 double-cut strategy (two sgRNAs flanking the locus releasing the donor in situ from a circular plasmid) gives 2–5× higher HDR rates for large inserts.
1 — Clone gRNA into PX458
Step 1Design & order ssOligo insert
Order from IDT as Ultramer DNA Oligo (recommended for sequences >60 nt)
  1. Design your 20 bp gRNA spacer. Verify PAM (NGG) and assess scores using Benchling or CRISPOR.
  2. Design an ssOligo with: 5′ homology arm (25 bp) matching PX458 upstream of BbsI cut · gRNA spacer (20 bp) · 3′ homology arm (25 bp) matching PX458 downstream of BbsI cut.
  3. Dissolve lyophilised ssOligo in nuclease-free water to 1 µM working stock.
Step 2BbsI Linearisation of PX458
BbsI Digest Calculator — PX458 Linearisation (20 µL reaction)
NEB BbsI (R0593): 37°C × 30 min in CutSmart Buffer | Target: 1 µg DNA in 20 µL total
Step 3HiFi Assembly & Transformation
NEBuilder HiFi DNA Assembly Master Mix (NEB E2621)
HiFi Assembly Calculator — PX458 + ssOligo (20 µL reaction)
Formula: pmol/µL = (ng/µL × 1000) / (bp × 660) | ssOligo insert:vector ratio = 200:1
Export protocol steps
  1. Set up the reaction on ice using the volumes from the calculator. Add components in order: water → vector → ssOligo → master mix
  2. Incubate at 50°C for 60 min in a thermocycler (lid off or 55°C)
  3. Transform 2 µL into NEB Stable E. coli (C3040H). Plate on LB + ampicillin (100 µg/mL) overnight 37°C
  4. Pick 6–8 colonies; miniprep; verify by Sanger sequencing across the cloning junction
2 — Plasmid donor design
  1. Clone: Left HA (400–800 bp) — Insert — Right HA (400–800 bp) into a donor backbone. HAs must be perfectly homologous to the genomic locus (verify with sequencing).
  2. Silence the PAM in the donor (1–2 silent mutations in the seed region) to prevent Cas9 from re-cutting after HDR.
  3. Add a silent diagnostic SNP ~10–15 bp from the cut site to distinguish the HDR allele from WT by sequencing.
  4. Zhang 2017 double-cut: design a second sgRNA on the opposite side of the insert; the two cuts release the insert+HAs from the circular plasmid. Use the same PX458 backbone or a separate gRNA plasmid.
  5. Maxiprep both PX458 and donor (endotoxin-free). A260/280 ≥ 1.8; A260/230 ≥ 2.0.
3 — Transfection (Lipofectamine 3000)
  1. Seed cells at 60–70% confluency the day before in a 6-well plate or 10 cm dish.
  2. Transfect: PX458 1.5 µg + plasmid donor 3–4 µg per well (ratio ~1:2–3 Cas9:donor). Use Lipofectamine 3000 per manufacturer protocol in Opti-MEM.
  3. Incubate 24–48 h. Check GFP by fluorescence microscopy — expect 20–60% GFP⁺ in transfectable lines.
  4. Do not select with antibiotics during the first 48 h post-transfection.
4 — FACS sorting & clonal expansion
  1. 48 h post-transfection: trypsinise, resuspend in sorting buffer (PBS + 2% FBS + 10 mM HEPES, filter 0.22 µm).
  2. Sort GFP⁺ cells (488 nm excitation). Gate top 10–20% GFP⁺ for enrichment of Cas9-expressing cells.
  3. Single-cell deposit into 96-well plates pre-loaded with 150 µL conditioned medium (50% fresh + 50% from the same cell line).
  4. See Single-Cell Sorting protocol for full clone expansion and genotyping workflow.
5 — Clone screening
  1. After 2–3 weeks expand colonies. Screen by junction PCR: one primer outside the HA, one inside the insert. A band indicates donor integration.
  2. Sanger-sequence PCR product across both junctions (5ʹ and 3ʹ) and the full insert.
  3. Assess zygosity: run both junction PCRs + a WT-allele PCR. Heterozygous clones are common — confirm homozygosity by allele-specific sequencing or digital PCR.
  4. Validate protein by Western blot, co-IP, or immunofluorescence before functional use.
Knock-in — RNP Electroporation + ssODN Apr 2026 ▶
Strategy: Pre-assembled Cas9 RNP (Cas9 protein + annealed crRNA:tracrRNA) is electroporated together with a single-stranded oligodeoxynucleotide (ssODN) donor. HDR incorporates the ssODN precisely at the cut site. Ideal for small edits: point mutations, epitope tags (HA, FLAG, V5), short linkers, or loxP sites (<200 bp total insert). No plasmid cloning needed — fast turnaround. Works best with Neon (Thermo) or 4D-Nucleofector (Lonza). Lower off-target and random integration risk vs. plasmid delivery.
1 — ssODN design
  1. Total length 100–200 nt. Flanking HAs of 40–80 nt each side of the edit. Asymmetric HAs (longer on the PAM-distal side) can improve HDR frequency.
  2. Silence the PAM in the ssODN to prevent re-cutting. Introduce a silent diagnostic SNP ~10 bp from the cut site.
  3. Order on the non-template strand (strand not used by Cas9 as template for nicking) — often gives higher HDR.
  4. Standard desalted synthesis sufficient for ≤120 nt; PAGE-purify if >120 nt. Resuspend in TE pH 8.0 at 100 µM.
2 — RNP assembly (IDT Alt-R system)
  1. Resuspend crRNA and tracrRNA to 200 µM in nuclease-free IDT Duplex Buffer.
  2. Mix equimolar crRNA + tracrRNA; anneal at 95 °C for 5 min, then cool slowly to RT in a heat block (turn off heater). Final gRNA duplex: 100 µM.
  3. Mix gRNA duplex + Cas9 protein at 1:1 molar ratio (e.g. 1 µL 61 µM Cas9 + 1 µL 100 µM gRNA → dilute to working [RNP] ~3 µM in PBS). Incubate 10–15 min at RT.
  4. Add ssODN separately — do not pre-mix with RNP more than a few seconds before electroporation.
3 — Electroporation
  1. Harvest cells in log phase (70–80% confluency). Viability must be >90% before electroporation.
  2. Pellet 1–5 × 10⁵ cells at 300 × g for 5 min. Wash once with PBS (no Ca²⁺/Mg²⁺). Remove all residual PBS.
  3. Resuspend in electroporation buffer: Neon: Buffer R (10 µL tip) or Buffer T; 4D: SF or SE buffer (20 µL) per cell-line optimisation guide.
  4. Add RNP (1–2 µL, ~100–200 pmol Cas9) + ssODN (1–2 µL, 100–200 pmol) to the cell suspension. Mix gently — do not vortex.
  5. Neon parameters (HEK293T/HCT116): 1400–1500 V · 20 ms · 1 pulse. Optimise per cell type.
    4D-Nucleofector: use manufacturer pre-set code for your cell line (e.g. CM-130 for HEK293).
  6. Transfer immediately to pre-warmed antibiotic-free medium. Do not disturb for 10 min.
4 — Recovery & clonal expansion
  1. 24 h post-electroporation: check viability and refresh medium. Expect 50–80% viability.
  2. 48–72 h: harvest a small aliquot for gDNA extraction. Run ICE analysis (Synthego) or T7E1 to confirm RNP activity — expect 20–60% indels as proxy for cutting efficiency.
  3. Single-cell sort or serially dilute into 96-well plates for clonal expansion.
  4. See Single-Cell Sorting protocol for full clone expansion and genotyping workflow.
5 — Genotyping HDR clones
  1. Design PCR primers outside the ssODN HA region to avoid amplifying residual ssODN template. Expected amplicon 300–600 bp.
  2. Run PCR product on gel to detect size difference (if insert ≥20 bp). Sequence all clones regardless.
  3. Sanger-sequence to confirm the exact edit. Check both alleles — heterozygous clones are common with ssODN HDR.
  4. Validate protein expression and function by Western blot and/or co-IP before use in experiments.
Single Cell Sorting (FACS) 14 Aug 2024 ▶
What is this? After CRISPR editing, the pool contains a mixture of edited and unedited cells. Single-cell sorting by FACS isolates individual cells into 96-well plates so that each well expands into a clonal population with a defined, homogeneous genotype. Conditioned media (collected from actively growing cells) provides secreted growth factors that improve single-cell viability during the critical early expansion phase.
Make Conditioned Media
  1. Grow T75 flasks to 60–70% confluency in log phase; remove all media without disturbing cells
  2. Store in aliquots at 4°C (few weeks) or −20°C (long-term)
  3. Before use: spin 4000 rpm × 5 min, discard pellet; filter through 0.22 um filter
  4. Dilute 1:1 with 20% FBS media (no antibiotics)
24 h Before Sorting — Prepare 96-well Plates
150 uL/well = 40% conditioned media + 40% DMEM + 20% FBS
For 6 plates (~95 mL): 38 mL conditioned + 38 mL DMEM + 19 mL FBS
  1. Aliquot 150 uL/well, leave at 37°C, 5% CO₂
1–2 h Before Sorting
  1. FACS media: 10 mL media + 100 uL FBS + 1 uL DAPI; filter through 0.22 um
30 min Before Sorting
  1. Trypsinize, pellet, resuspend at 5×10⁶/mL in media + 1% FBS (max 300 uL if <2M cells)
At SLS Sorting Facility
Bring: cell samples in 15 mL falcon · 10 mL media + 1% FBS (with DAPI) · pre-prepared 96-well plates
  1. Spin plates in TC room centrifuge (must tilt 90°)
  2. Place at back of incubator 37°C × ~20 days — do NOT touch
Clone Expansion
  1. After 20 days: mark wells with visible white specks; check all wells under microscope
  2. Add 50 uL fresh media to chosen wells; resuspend cells to disperse across well
  3. If contamination suspected: transfer full volume to new 96-well plate
Cell Biology
Prep · Protein detection · Functional assays · Gene delivery · Tools
Prep Protein quantification · Lysis
Protocol Detection · Pulldown · Gene delivery
Assay Degradation · Viability readouts
Tools Visualisation aids
Cell Lines Culture conditions · Handling notes
BCA & Sample Prep — ▶
What is this? The BCA assay quantifies total protein in a lysate by colorimetric reduction of Cu²⁺ to Cu⁺ (biuret reaction), detected at 562 nm. The absorbance of each sample is interpolated against a BSA standard curve to give ug/uL. That concentration is then used to calculate how much lysate to take for a given protein mass, mixed with LDS loading buffer and reducing agent for SDS-PAGE.
1 — BCA Assay
  1. Prepare BSA standards: 2, 1, 0.5, 0.25, 0.125 mg/mL in ddH₂O — 25 uL/well in duplicate on a 96-well plate
  2. Dilute lysates: 1:6 (10 uL lysate + 50 uL ddH₂O) or 1:10 for very concentrated samples — 25 uL/well in duplicate
  3. Add 200 uL BCA reagent (50:1 A:B); mix at 600 rpm × 1 min; incubate 37°C × 30 min
  4. Remove bubbles with needle; read plate at 562 nm; ensure R² ≥ 0.99 for the standard curve
  5. Enter resulting concentrations into the calculator below
Remember to back-calculate the dilution factor — if you diluted 1:6, multiply the interpolated value × 6 to get the actual lysate concentration.
2 — Sample Prep Calculator
After normalising all samples to the same concentration in Excel, use this calculator to work out: how much LDS + reducing agent to prepare as a master mix, how much to add to each sample tube, and how much of the boiled mixture to load into each well to hit your protein target.
SDS-PAGE Sample Prep Calculator
Workflow: normalise lysates to fixed concentration (Excel) → add LDS + reducing agent master mix → boil 95°C × 5 min → load calculated volume per well
Gel (NuPAGE Bis-Tris pre-cast, Invitrogen)
Lysate
Buffers
Protein load
Exceeds maximum for current settings
Export protocol steps
Western Blot 24 Apr 2024 ▶
What is this? Western blotting (immunoblotting) detects specific proteins in a cell lysate by separating them by molecular weight (SDS-PAGE), transferring to a membrane, and probing with a primary antibody followed by an HRP-conjugated secondary. The result is a band at the expected molecular weight, providing information on protein expression level, size, and post-translational modification state.
Cell Lysis
  1. Optional: pre-cool centrifuge at 4°C
  2. Prepare lysis buffer on ice: RIPA (~50–100 uL/6-well; ~50 uL/12-well) + 1× PI (100× stock) + benzonase (1000×)
  3. Wash on ice: aspirate medium → cold PBS → aspirate PBS
  4. Add lysis buffer, distribute carefully; incubate 10 min in fridge (redistribute once)
  5. Scratch down lysate (tilted plate); transfer to Eppendorf
  6. Optional: centrifuge 4°C, 13,500 × g, 15 min; transfer supernatant
  7. Optional: sonicate (cold room, max potency, 5 s/sample, wipe with 70% EtOH between samples)
  8. Keep on ice for BCA or freeze at −80°C
BCA Protein Quantification
  1. BSA standards: 2, 1, 0.5, 0.25, 0.125 mg/mL in ddH₂O — 25 uL per well, in duplicate
  2. Dilute lysates 1:6 (10 uL + 50 uL ddH₂O) or 1:10 for concentrated samples
  3. Load 25 uL standard + sample in duplicate; add 200 uL BCA reagent (50:1 A:B); mix 600 rpm × 1 min; 37°C × 30 min
  4. Remove bubbles with needle; read at 562 nm; confirm R² ≥ 0.99
  5. Use the BCA & Sample Prep calculator (separate protocol) to calculate loading volumes from your BCA concentrations
SDS-PAGE
  1. Running buffer: 1× MOPS from 20× stock (1 L)
  2. NuPAGE 4–12% bis-tris pre-cast gel: remove white strip; remove comb after assembling; fill with running buffer; wells facing inwards
  3. Load ladder (2.5 uL); run 180 V × 1h 15 min (20-well gel)
Transfer & Blotting
Transfer buffer: 10% 10× Transfer Buffer + 10% EtOH + 80% ddH₂O (reusable)
TBS-T: 100 mL 10× TBS + 900 mL ddH₂O + 1 mL Tween 20
5% Milk-TBS-T: 2.5 g dried milk in 50 mL TBS-T
Sandwich: BLACK → Sponge → Filter paper ×3 → Gel → Membrane → Filter paper ×3 → Sponge → CLEAR
  1. Cut nitrocellulose membrane + Whatman filter paper to 7×9 cm; pre-wet sponges and filter paper
  2. Assemble sandwich (black to black); run 90 V × 90 min in ice-cold tank
  3. Ponceau × 1 min rocking; rinse with tap water; confirm even loading
  4. Wash TBS-T × 5 min; block 5% milk TBS-T × 1 h RT; rinse TBS-T × 5 s
  5. Primary antibody in 5% milk TBS-T overnight at 4°C; keep antibody for reuse (freeze)
Next Day
  1. Wash 2× TBS-T × 5–10 min
  2. Secondary: α-Ms/Rb (1:4000) + α-tubulin hFAB rhodamine (1:4000) in TBS-T; 1 h RT on rocker
  3. Wash 3–5× TBS-T × 5–10 min; image
Pip Tip
For better signal-to-noise with phospho-antibodies, use BSA instead of milk for blocking and antibody incubation — milk contains casein (a phosphoprotein) which can interfere with phospho-specific antibodies.
Immunoprecipitation 24 Mar 2025 ▶
What is this? Immunoprecipitation (IP) uses a specific antibody to pull down a target protein — along with any interacting partners — from a cell lysate. The antibody-protein complex is captured on Protein G/A magnetic beads, washed to remove non-specific binders, and eluted for analysis by western blot or mass spectrometry. Used to study protein–protein interactions, confirm antibody specificity, and assess complex formation.
Seeding & Treatment
  1. Seed one 10 cm plate per condition: 1.2M cells/plate
  2. Optional: seed extra plate/condition for MS analysis
  3. Treat cells; start with 1 h compound incubation
Lysis
  1. Wash with PBS; add RIPA (300 uL/well) + 1× PI (100×) + benzonase (1000×)
  2. Incubate 10 min in fridge; redistribute once
  3. Scrape, transfer to Eppendorf; centrifuge 13,000 rpm × 15 min; keep supernatant
  4. BCA quantification; normalize to ~3 mg/sample
Immunoprecipitation (RBM39 IgG sc-376531 example)
  1. 200 ug sample → split: 100 ug INPUT + 100 ug IP
  2. IP lysate → 2 tubes: Tube 1 — RBM39 IgG (2 ug/250 ug protein; 40 uL/mg if at 200 ug/mL); Tube 2 — Ms IgG control (1 ug/mg lysate)
  3. Add RIPA to 1 mL total; incubate overnight 4°C with rotation
Bead Capture
  1. Wash Protein G/A beads (25 uL/sample): 500 uL wash buffer (50 mM Tris + 150 mM NaCl) × 2, magnetic rack
  2. Add overnight samples to beads; incubate 1 h 4°C with rotation
  3. Collect flow-through on magnetic rack (keep for analysis)
  4. Wash 5× with 1000 uL wash buffer
Elution
  1. Add 80 uL loading buffer (4× LDS + 10× DTT); boil 90°C × 10 min; collect supernatant on magnet
  2. Run on SDS gel → Western blot
DTT elution dissociates antibody into light + heavy chains (~25 and ~50 kDa) — both will be detected by α-Ms secondary. To avoid: use different species for IP vs WB primary antibody.
HiBiT Blotting System (Promega) 26 Feb 2025 ▶
What is this? HiBiT is an 11-amino-acid tag that, when expressed in cells or detected on a blot, reconstitutes NanoLuc luciferase upon addition of the LgBiT protein and substrate. On a western blot, this replaces the need for a primary + secondary antibody — LgBiT binds HiBiT directly and produces chemiluminescent signal. It is faster, more quantitative, and more sensitive than standard antibody-based WB for tagged proteins.
Promega HiBiT Blotting System Protocol
  1. Separate proteins by SDS-PAGE (include prestained MW markers)
  2. Transfer to nitrocellulose membrane (iBlot or wet transfer)
  3. Rinse membrane in TBS-T (removes transfer buffer; solubilizes HiBiT tag); keep in TBS-T or water until step 6
  4. Prepare Nano-Glo® HiBiT reagent: dilute 10× Nano-Glo® Blotting Buffer in water → add LgBiT (200×) → add Nano-Glo® Luciferase Substrate (500×)
  5. Incubate membrane × 5 min with gentle rocking
  6. Image with chemiluminescence imager — keep membrane in solution for continuous binding
Pip Tip
Unlike standard WB, HiBiT does not require primary or secondary antibody steps — LgBiT binds HiBiT complementarily to reconstitute NanoLuc luciferase. This makes it extremely fast and quantitative. Can be combined with standard WB on the same membrane (image HiBiT first, then strip and re-probe with antibodies).
HiBiT Lytic Assay — Reagent Calculator — ▶
What is this? The HiBiT Lytic Assay measures intracellular HiBiT-tagged protein levels by lysing cells directly in the plate with Nano-Glo® HiBiT Lytic Detection Reagent. LgBiT and substrate are added simultaneously in the lytic buffer — no washing, no transfer. Luminescence signal is proportional to HiBiT-tagged protein abundance. Used routinely to quantify degrader-induced target degradation in 96- or 384-well format.
Promega Nano-Glo HiBiT Lytic Detection System
Protocol
  1. Treat cells with compounds in the plate (cells already seeded in assay volume). Allow compound incubation as required (typically 4–24 h for degraders).
  2. Prepare HiBiT Lytic Detection Reagent as calculated below: add LgBiT and substrate to Nano-Glo® HiBiT Lytic Buffer in a single tube. Mix gently, avoid vortexing.
  3. Add the calculated volume of lytic reagent directly to each well. Mix by plate shaker (300 rpm, 1 min).
  4. Incubate at room temperature for 10 min with shaking.
  5. Measure luminescence on GloMax, PHERAstar, or EnVision. Integration time: 0.3 s per well.
Lytic Reagent Calculator
Export protocol steps
CellTiter-Glo® 2.0 — Cell Viability 13 Apr 2026 ▶
What is this? CellTiter-Glo® 2.0 (Promega) is a homogeneous, luminescent cell viability assay that quantifies ATP as a proxy for metabolically active cells. The single-reagent “add-mix-measure” format lyses cells and generates a stable glow-type signal (t½ > 3 h) proportional to viable cell number. Ideal for 96-, 384-, or 1536-well HTS formats and for measuring degrader-induced cytotoxicity.
📄 Promega CellTiter-Glo® 2.0 Technical Manual (TM403)
Reagent Preparation
  1. If frozen, thaw CellTiter-Glo® 2.0 Reagent at 4°C overnight — or in a 22°C water bath (do not exceed 25°C; if stored at −65°C, leave on bench 10–15 min before water-bath transfer to avoid cracking)
  2. Equilibrate to room temperature in a 22°C water bath — 100 mL requires ~30 min; 500 mL requires ~100 min
  3. Mix gently by inversion to homogenise — use caution removing the seal to avoid ATP contamination
Cell Viability Protocol
  1. Plate cells in opaque-walled multiwell plates in culture medium; optimise cell number and volume per format
  2. Optional: prepare blank wells (medium only, no cells) to determine background luminescence
  3. Add test compound to experimental wells; incubate per your assay protocol
  4. Equilibrate plate to room temperature for ~30 min
  5. Add CellTiter-Glo® 2.0 Reagent equal in volume to the cell culture medium per well (e.g. 96-well: 100 uL reagent to 100 uL medium; 384-well: 25 uL to 25 uL)
  6. Mix on orbital shaker 2 min to induce cell lysis
  7. Incubate at room temperature for 10 min to stabilise the luminescent signal
  8. Record luminescence — integration time 0.25–1 s/well
Opaque-walled plates are essential — clear-well plates cause optical crosstalk between wells. Use white plates for maximum signal, black for low-background in multiplexed formats.
Pip Tip
For degrader cytotoxicity (Dmax studies), seed cells 24 h before compound addition, treat 72 h, then run the assay. Normalise to DMSO control. The extended half-life (>3 h) means you can read multiple plates in a single batch — no injector needed. CellTiter-Glo® 2.0 can be multiplexed with CellTox™ Green (measure fluorescence first, then add CTG2 reagent).
Reagent Volume Calculator
CellTiter-Glo® 2.0 Reagent Calculator
Promega TM403 — equal volume of CTG2 reagent per well; stable at 4°C ≤2 months after thaw
Retro/Lentiviral Transduction 07 Apr 2025 ▶
What is this? Retro/lentiviral transduction delivers a gene of interest into target cells via viral particles produced in HEK293FT packaging cells. Unlike transient transfection, the viral cargo integrates into the host genome, giving stable, heritable expression. Retroviruses integrate only in dividing cells; lentiviruses can infect non-dividing cells. Polybrene is added during infection to neutralise charge repulsion between the viral envelope and cell membrane.
Virus Generation
Steps 1–6: do NOT use Pen/Strep in the media
  1. Plate HEK293FT at 1.5×10⁵ cells/mL in 10 mL into 10 cm dish
  2. Next day (~60% confluency): prepare transfection mix:
    Tube 1: 300 uL OptiMEM + 6 ug pBabe/pRetroX + 3.8 ug Clontech GAG/POL + 2 ug Clontech VSVG
    Tube 2: 300 uL OptiMEM + 24 uL PEI
  3. Incubate 5 min in hood; combine tubes, mix gently, incubate 20 min
  4. Add to 9 mL pre-warmed complete media; mix gently
  5. Replace media on HEK293FT; incubate overnight
  6. Next morning: replace with fresh 10 mL; incubate 24 h
Harvest (24 h after media change)
  1. Remove media; centrifuge 2100 × g × 5 min; filter through 0.45 um; use or store at −80°C
Maximize yield: collect at 48, 72, 96 h post-transfection, replacing with fresh media; pool all before filtering.
Viral Infection
  1. Seed 3 wells per plasmid in 6-well plate overnight (0.5M cells/well, 2 mL media)
  2. Prepare 3 media tubes: (a) virus only, (b) virus:media 1:1, (c) media only
  3. Add Polybrene to 8 ug/mL (1.6 uL/2 mL); add to each well; incubate ~24 h
  4. Replace with selection antibiotic at 48–72 h post-transduction
  5. Recover after selection; expand; after 3 passages, transfer to CL1 incubators
Transfection Calculator — ▶
Per-well reagent amounts and step-by-step protocol for common transfection reagents. Includes 10% overage by default.
Transfection Setup
Gel Diagram Generator — ▶
What is this? Generates a labelled diagram of your gel layout — well types, sample labels, concentration series, and grouping brackets — and exports it as a PNG for OneNote, lab notebooks, or figures.
Gel Layout Designer
Wells (Tab to advance)
Brackets
Preview
Copied!
Proteomics
S-Trap sample prep · DIA-NN · Perseus statistical analysis
Prep MS-grade sample preparation
Analysis Data processing · Statistical analysis
S-Trap Sample Preparation 25 Feb 2025 ▶
What is this? The S-Trap (suspension trapping) column enables rapid, efficient sample preparation for bottom-up proteomics. Proteins are reduced, alkylated, and loaded onto the S-Trap column in a high-methanol binding buffer — this simultaneously concentrates the protein and removes interfering detergents (SDS, RIPA components). Trypsin is added directly on-column for digestion, and resulting peptides are eluted for LC-MS/MS analysis.
Reduction
  1. DTT vial (7.7 mg) + 100 uL H₂O → 500 mM stock; add volume for 20 mM final in each tube
  2. Incubate 55°C × 20–30 min
Alkylation
  1. 200 mM TEAB from 1 M stock; add 132 uL to alkylator vial → 375 mM; add for 20 mM final
  2. Incubate 10 min RT in the dark (cover tubes)
Acidify
  1. Add 1:10 of 12% phosphoric acid (e.g. 5 uL to 45 uL sample)
Trap Protein on S-Trap
  1. Binding/Wash buffer: 1 mL TEAB 1M + 9 mL MeOH (100 mM TEAB in 90% MeOH)
  2. Add 7× sample volume of buffer; mix; transfer entirely to S-Trap in 1.5 mL tube
  3. Centrifuge 4000 × g × 30 s; if not fully passed through, centrifuge again (up to 15,000 × g for clogs)
Wash × 3
  1. Add 150 uL Binding/Wash buffer; centrifuge 4000 × g × 30 s; discard flow-through; repeat ×3
  2. Final centrifuge 4000 × g × 1 min; transfer S-Trap to fresh tube
Trypsin Digestion
  1. Ratio 1:15 (recommended): 100 ug protein → 6.67 ug trypsin; mix 100 ug trypsin in 1500 uL 100 mM TEAB → add 100 uL to each S-Trap
  2. Spin to pass trypsin through; recover and add back
  3. Cap loosely — do NOT seal airtight (pressure forces solution out)
  4. Incubate 37°C overnight
Elution
  1. 100 uL 50 mM TEAB → 4000 × g × 1 min
  2. 100 uL 0.15% formic acid → 4000 × g × 1 min
  3. 100 uL 50% ACN → 4000 × g × 1 min
  4. Transfer all to labelled tubes; take ~30 ug/sample; dry in SpeedVac (Proteomics bay)
Trypsin Digestion Calculator
Based on: S-Trap protocol — trypsin:protein 1:10 to 1:50 (recommended 1:15)
Export protocol steps
DIA-NN — Data-Independent Acquisition Analysis 13 Jan 2026 ▶
What is DIA-NN? DIA-NN is a software pipeline for processing data-independent acquisition (DIA) mass spectrometry data. Unlike DDA (data-dependent acquisition), DIA fragments all ions in a scan window simultaneously — this gives better quantitative reproducibility across samples. DIA-NN deconvolutes the resulting complex spectra by matching against in silico-predicted spectral libraries generated from a FASTA file. The output is a protein group quantification matrix ready for statistical analysis.
DIA-NN GitHub (Demichev lab) · v1.8 and v1.9 supported
Maintain strict sample order, labelling, and classification before you start — mislabelled samples will produce wrong differential comparisons downstream and are very hard to catch after the fact.
A — Data Organisation
  1. Create a new folder inside the RAW data directory for this experiment (e.g. Samples_1-20_COMPOUND_DATE)
  2. All DIA-NN outputs and downstream analyses must live in this folder
  3. Write down your sample order before opening DIA-NN — you will need it for Perseus annotation
B — FASTA File Preparation
  1. Use the human whole-proteome FASTA available on the computer room workstation (contaminants already added by Gajanan, copied to your user folder)
  2. If you have custom sequences to detect (knock-in tags, HiBiT, fluorescent proteins, exogenous constructs): open the FASTA in a text editor and add them manually in standard FASTA format:
    >sp|CUSTOM|MY_TAG_HUMAN
    MGSSHHHHHH...
  3. Why this matters: DIA-NN generates in silico tryptic peptides from this file and matches experimental MS2 spectra against them. Any protein not in the FASTA will not be quantified, regardless of how many peptides you have for it.
C — DIA-NN Processing Setup
  1. Load raw files: top left panel → RAW → Select all → Open (all .raw or .mzML files for this experiment)
  2. Add FASTA: click "Add FASTA" → select your prepared FASTA file
  3. Library generation: enable "FASTA digest for library-free search" — this generates the spectral library on the fly from your FASTA, no pre-built library needed. Enable deep learning-based spectra and RT prediction.
  4. General settings: leave all defaults unchanged
  5. MBR (Match Between Runs): ✅ ENABLE — this uses retention time alignment to transfer identifications between runs, dramatically improving quantitative completeness, especially for low-abundance proteins
  6. Missed cleavages: set to 1 (allows one missed trypsin cleavage site per peptide — increases sensitivity without too much noise)
  7. Output folder: set to the folder created in step A
  8. Click Run — runtime is ~4+ h for a full dataset
D — Key Output Files
  1. report.pg_matrix.tsv — main output: protein group quantification matrix. This is what you load into Perseus.
  2. report.tsv — precursor-level report, more granular, useful for troubleshooting
  3. report.stats.tsv — per-run QC stats: number of proteins identified, median CV, etc. Check this first to spot problematic runs before statistical analysis
Pip Tip
Pip Tip — v1.9 contaminant handling: DIA-NN 1.9 allows adding contaminants as a separate dedicated FASTA rather than embedding them in the main FASTA. You can use your own list or the built-in DIA-NN contaminant database. If using this approach, remove the contaminant filtering step from Perseus (step F below) — they will already be excluded at the DIA-NN level. Check with Gajanan which approach is set up on the workstation.
Perseus — Statistical Analysis of Proteomics Data 13 Jan 2026 ▶
What is Perseus? Perseus (Max Planck Institute) is the standard tool for statistical analysis of quantitative proteomics data. It takes the protein quantification matrix from DIA-NN and applies log transformation, filtering, imputation of missing values, and statistical tests (volcano plots, t-tests). The workflow below goes step by step — order matters.
Perseus download and documentation — Save your session frequently (Perseus crashes without warning).
A — Import Data
  1. Top left → Generic matrix upload
  2. Select report.pg_matrix.tsv from your DIA-NN output folder
  3. Text format is auto-detected — confirm
  4. Select all sample columns → assign to "Main" (these are your quantitative intensity values)
  5. Click OK → Save As immediately (File → Save As) with a descriptive name. Do this before any processing.
B — Sample Annotation
  1. Go to Annotation rows (top menu)
  2. Add categorical annotation → assign group labels per sample column (e.g. Samples 1–4 → "Compound_1", Samples 5–8 → "DMSO")
  3. Why this matters: Perseus uses these group labels to define comparisons in the volcano plot and t-test. Mislabelling here means wrong statistics — double-check against your sample list from DIA-NN.
  4. Tick the checkbox → OK
C — Log₂ Transformation
  1. Top left → Basic → Apply log₂ transform to all Main columns
  2. Why: raw intensity values span many orders of magnitude and are not normally distributed. Log₂ transformation compresses the range, making distributions more symmetric and variance more homogeneous — required for t-tests and PCA to be valid. A 1-unit change in log₂ = 2-fold change in abundance.
D — Filter Contaminants and Low-Quality IDs
Why filter before imputing? Imputation fills in missing values by sampling from a noise distribution. If you impute before filtering, you introduce noise-based values for proteins that were never real identifications (contaminants, decoys). Filter first, then impute.
  1. Processing → Filter rows → Filter rows based on categorical column
  2. Remove Reverse (+) — decoy/reverse-sequence hits, not real protein IDs
  3. Remove Only identified by site (+) — lower-confidence identifications supported only by modified peptides
  4. Remove Contaminants (+) — common sample prep contaminants (keratins, trypsin, albumin…)
  5. Note: these columns come from MaxQuant output. If loading DIA-NN output directly, these columns may not exist — skip this step if absent.
E — Filter by Valid Values
  1. Processing → Filter rows → Filter rows based on valid values
  2. Recommended setting: minimum 2 valid values in at least one group — keeps proteins quantified in at least one condition, even if missing in the other (useful for condition-specific hits)
  3. Avoid requiring valid values in all groups — this discards many real hits that are genuinely absent in one condition (e.g. degraded proteins)
  4. Optional: additionally filter by Peptides > 2 (removes proteins supported by only 1–2 peptides, which are less reliable quantitatively)
F — Missing Value Imputation
  1. Top left → Imputation → Replace missing values from normal distribution
  2. Leave default settings (downshift = 1.8, width = 0.3) → OK
  3. Why impute? Statistical tests (t-test, ANOVA) require complete matrices. Proteins missing in one condition but quantified in another are real biological signals — imputing from the low end of the distribution (simulating "below detection limit") allows these to appear as significant hits in the volcano plot. This is the standard approach in proteomics.
G — Exploratory Analysis: PCA (Optional but Recommended)
  1. Top middle → Principal Component Analysis
  2. Replicates should cluster tightly together; different conditions should separate
  3. An outlier replicate that clusters away from its group is a sign of a problem (failed sample prep, labelling error, degraded sample) — consider excluding it and re-running the analysis
  4. PCA doesn't change your data, just helps you interpret it before committing to statistical comparisons
H — Differential Analysis: Volcano Plot
  1. Top middle → Volcano Plot
  2. Define comparison: e.g. "Compound_1" vs "DMSO" using your categorical annotation
  3. Settings: t-test type = Student's t-test (standard); FDR = 0.05; S0 = 0.1 (controls the curve shape — increase to be more stringent)
  4. Proteins in the upper right (enriched in compound) or upper left (depleted/degraded) above the significance curve are your hits
  5. For degradation experiments: proteins depleted in the compound condition (left side) with large fold change are your degraded proteins
I — Export Results
  1. Right-click on any protein in the volcano → Export → Plain matrix
  2. Save as .txt; open in Excel for further annotation
  3. Also export the full processed matrix: File → Export → Generic matrix export → .txt
Pip Tip
For degradation proteomics, you typically care about proteins that are specifically depleted (left side of volcano) with statistical significance. A clean degrader will show your POI as the most significantly depleted protein with the largest fold change. Co-depleted proteins can indicate on-target collateral effects (complex members, interactors) or off-target degradation — worth following up. The S0 parameter in the volcano t-test controls the minimum fold change required for significance; increase it if you want to be more stringent about fold change.
Genomics & Bioinformatics
MiSeq amplicon sequencing · Gene retrieval & annotation
Protocol NGS · Sequencing
Analysis Sequence retrieval · Annotation
MiSeq — Amplicon Sequencing 16 Jan 2025 ▶
What is this? MiSeq amplicon sequencing generates short paired-end reads (2×300 bp) from a specific PCR-amplified locus across many samples simultaneously. In the context of CRISPR editing, it quantifies editing outcomes (indel spectra, HDR efficiency, allele frequencies) at the target site with single-nucleotide resolution across thousands of reads per sample — far more informative than Sanger sequencing.
protocols.io (Springer Nature)
1 — Design Primers
  1. Design amplicon-specific primers around site of interest; optimal amplicon ~200 bp (excl. universal tag); site should be central in the amplicon
  2. Avoid non-coding regions (higher SNP density)
  3. Tool: NCBI Primer-Blast (~300 bp template, set range so site is central)
  4. Add Illumina Nextera XT overhangs:
    Fwd: TCGTCGGCAGCGTCAGATGTGTATAAGAGACAG + your primer
    Rev: GTCTCGTGGGCTCGGAGATGTGTATAAGAGACAG + your primer
  5. Optional: test for dimers/hairpins with IDT OligoAnalyzer
  6. Order as standard desalted; reconstitute to 100 uM stock (10× nMoles uL of NFW), working at 10 uM
2 — Test PCR (GoTAQ G2, Optional)
GoTAQ G2 MasterMix 2× (Promega #M7832) — 25 uL: 7.5 uL H₂O + 12.5 uL 2× GoTAQ + 1.5 uL 10 uM Fwd + 1.5 uL 10 uM Rev + 2 uL template (QuickExtract)
Programme: 95°C 1 min → [95°C 30 s → 60°C 30 s → 72°C 10 s] ×30 → 72°C 5 min → 4°C
  1. Run 10 uL on 1% TAE agarose gel — expect 267 bp for 200 bp amplicon
3 — High-Fidelity PCR for MiSeq Submission
Platinum SuperFi II (Thermo #12368010) — 50 uL: 15 uL H₂O + 25 uL 2× SuperFi II + 2.5 uL 10 uM Fwd + 2.5 uL 10 uM Rev + 5 uL template
Programme: 98°C 30 s → [98°C 10 s → Tm* 10 s → 72°C 15 s] ×30 → 72°C 5 min → 4°C (*Tm of your primer pair)
  1. Set up 2 replicate reactions per sample; pool afterwards
  2. Run 10 uL on 1% TAE agarose gel to confirm single band
4 — Clean Up (AMPure XP Beads)
  1. Equilibrate beads to RT; vortex 10 s; add 0.8:1 bead:sample ratio
  2. Incubate RT × 8 min; magnetic separator × 5 min; remove supernatant
  3. Add 200 uL fresh 80% EtOH × 2 washes (30 s each on magnet); remove EtOH
  4. Air dry until shiny disappears but before cracks appear; add 30 uL molecular-grade H₂O; incubate 2 min RT; transfer supernatant
5 — Submission
  1. Check concentration: NanoDrop or Qubit
  2. Submit ≥6 uL at 15–25 ng/uL; label: name + date + "For MiSeq"
MiSeq PCR Setup Calculator
Based on: SuperFi II 50 uL HiFi reaction + GoTAQ 25 uL test reaction
Export protocol steps
Retrieve & Annotate Gene Sequences (NCBI + SnapGene) 18 Oct 2024 ▶
What is this? Before any cloning or CRISPR experiment, you need the correct, annotated sequence of your gene locus. This protocol retrieves the RefSeq record from NCBI, identifies the canonical transcript and CDS, and imports it into SnapGene for visual annotation — establishing the "source of truth" sequence used for all downstream primer design, donor design, and interpretation of sequencing results.
1 — Obtain Gene Sequence (NCBI)
  1. Go to NCBI Gene; enter gene name + organism (e.g. "DCAF15 human")
  2. Select RefSeq entry (e.g. NM_XXXXX); note accession number
  3. Scroll to transcript sequence and exon information; copy entire transcript
2 — Import into SnapGene
  1. File → New DNA Sequence; paste sequence → OK
  2. Sequence View: mark 5' UTR, CDS, 3' UTR based on base positions from NCBI
  3. Highlight exon/intron junctions; mark signal peptide if present
3 — Retrieve Promoter (EPD)
  1. Go to Eukaryotic Promoter Database (EPD); enter gene ID + organism
  2. Retrieve promoter sequence upstream of TSS; look for TATA box ~30 bp upstream (may be absent)
4 — Transcription Factor Binding Sites
  1. Use PROMO; select species; paste promoter sequence; review TF list
5 — Final Annotation in SnapGene
  1. Combine annotated gene + promoter sequences in SnapGene; save for further analysis
Pip Tip
For CRISPR target sites, once you have the SnapGene file, the Benchling CRISPR tool can identify all Cas9/Cpf1 target sites genome-wide and score off-target effects directly on your annotated sequence — useful complement to IDT for guide selection.
Structural Biology
PDB · AlphaFold · PyMOL · Binding sites · Ternary complexes
Reference Structure databases · Prediction
Tools Visualisation · Publication figures
Analysis Binding sites · Ternary complexes
Why structure matters in chemical biology: Before designing a PROTAC, inhibitor, or degrader, you want to understand the 3D shape of your target — where ligands bind, which residues are important, whether a surface is druggable, and how two proteins might interact. Structural data is your map. Even an imperfect map (AlphaFold) is far better than no map at all.
Finding & Evaluating Crystal Structures (PDB) RCSB PDB ▶
What is the PDB? The Protein Data Bank (PDB) is a public archive of experimentally determined 3D structures of proteins, nucleic acids, and complexes. Each entry is identified by a 4-character code (e.g. 6HAX). Structures are determined by X-ray crystallography, cryo-EM, or NMR. Not all structures are equal — resolution, completeness, and experimental conditions matter.
1 — Search for Your Protein
  1. Go to rcsb.org — search by protein name, gene name, UniProt ID, or ligand (e.g. "CRBN", "VHL", "BRD4")
  2. Filter results by: Organism (prefer Homo sapiens) · Experimental method (X-ray or cryo-EM) · Resolution (lower Å = better; aim for ≤2.5 Å for atomic detail)
  3. Sort by resolution — the best structure is usually at the top, but check it has a ligand if you want to see the binding pocket
2 — Evaluate a Structure Before Using It
Why evaluate? A structure in the PDB is not automatically "good". Check these before building conclusions on it.
  1. Resolution: the number in Å. <2.0 Å = excellent; 2.0–2.5 Å = good; 2.5–3.5 Å = usable but be careful; >3.5 Å = low confidence on side-chain positions
  2. R-factor / R-free: shown on the PDB page under "Refinement". R-free <0.25 is acceptable; big difference between R and R-free suggests overfitting
  3. Completeness: check if your region of interest has missing residues (shown as gaps in the sequence viewer) — disordered loops are often absent
  4. Ligand: if the structure has your ligand of interest, check the electron density around it — on the PDB page, click "3D View" and look for the green mesh around the ligand. No mesh = no confidence
  5. Biological assembly: some proteins are dimers or tetramers — check the "Biological Assembly" tab, not just the asymmetric unit
3 — Download the Structure
  1. Click Download Files (top right of any PDB entry) → choose PDB Format (.pdb) for PyMOL, or mmCIF for ChimeraX/Coot
  2. For large structures or cryo-EM maps, the mmCIF format is preferred
  3. Save with a meaningful name: e.g. CRBN_lenalidomide_6HAX.pdb
4 — Useful PDB Tools
  1. RCSB Pairwise Structure Alignment — compare two structures online without PyMOL
  2. PDBe-KB — aggregates all structures for a given UniProt entry; shows coverage across the sequence; great starting point
  3. UniProt → Structure tab — links directly to all PDB entries for your protein, with sequence coverage shown visually
Pip Tip
For PROTAC/degrader design, you ideally want structures of both the target protein (POI) and the E3 ligase (e.g. CRBN or VHL) with their respective ligands bound. This lets you think about where the linker exits each binding pocket and which solvent-exposed vectors are available for elaboration. The RCSB Advanced Search lets you filter by "Has Ligand" to find co-crystal structures directly.
AlphaFold — Structure Prediction DeepMind / EBI ▶
What is AlphaFold? AlphaFold2 (DeepMind, 2021) predicts protein 3D structure from amino acid sequence alone with remarkable accuracy. It doesn't tell you about ligand binding or dynamics, but it gives you a starting model when no crystal structure exists. The EBI hosts pre-computed models for essentially the entire human proteome — free to download in seconds.
When to Use AlphaFold vs a Crystal Structure
Always prefer an experimental structure (PDB) over AlphaFold when one exists at good resolution, especially around the binding site. Crystal structures capture real ligand-induced conformational changes; AlphaFold does not model bound ligands and may miss binding-relevant conformations.
  1. Use AlphaFold when: no PDB structure exists for your protein · you need a model of a domain not covered by any crystal structure · you want to predict the fold of a mutant or tagged construct
  2. Use a crystal structure when: you want to understand ligand binding · you need accurate side-chain positions in the binding pocket · you're doing docking
1 — AlphaFold Database (Pre-computed Models)
  1. Go to alphafold.ebi.ac.uk
  2. Search by protein name or UniProt ID (e.g. "DCAF15" or "Q9NWF9")
  3. Download the .pdb file from the page — this is your AlphaFold model
  4. The page also shows the pLDDT score mapped onto the structure — read this before trusting any region (see below)
2 — Understanding pLDDT (Confidence Score)
pLDDT (predicted Local Distance Difference Test) runs from 0–100 and is colour-coded on the model:
🔵 Dark blue (>90): very high confidence — trust this backbone and most side chains
🟦 Light blue (70–90): good confidence — backbone reliable, some side-chain uncertainty
🟡 Yellow (50–70): low confidence — backbone rough, side chains unreliable. Often intrinsically disordered regions.
🟠 Orange (<50): very low confidence — likely disordered or a flexible loop. Do not interpret these regions structurally.
3 — AlphaFold3 / ColabFold for Custom Predictions
  1. AlphaFold Server (AF3) — predict structures of proteins, protein–protein complexes, or protein–small molecule complexes. Free, no installation. Log in with Google. Submit sequence(s) and wait ~10–30 min.
  2. ColabFold (Google Colab) — faster, also free, good for multimers. Useful when AF3 server queue is long.
  3. For a ternary complex (POI + E3 ligase), you can submit both sequences to AF3 as a multimer — the PAE (Predicted Aligned Error) plot tells you how confident the interface prediction is
4 — PAE Plot (for multimers)
PAE (Predicted Aligned Error) is shown as a 2D matrix when you predict a complex. Low PAE between two chains (dark green squares off the diagonal) = AlphaFold is confident about the relative positioning of those two chains = credible interface prediction. High PAE = uncertain relative positioning — treat the interface with caution.
Pip Tip
AlphaFold3 can now include small molecule ligands (cofactors, metals) in predictions. For PROTAC design, you can submit POI + CRBN/VHL + a SMILES string of your compound to get a rough ternary complex model. These are speculative but useful for early-stage linker vector thinking. Don't over-interpret side-chain positions in the predicted interface.
PyMOL — Getting Started (Visualisation Basics) Schrödinger PyMOL ▶
What is PyMOL? PyMOL is the standard tool for 3D protein structure visualisation. It loads .pdb files and lets you explore the structure, colour residues, show surfaces, measure distances, and make publication-quality figures. It has both a GUI (click-based) and a command line — learning a few commands makes it much faster than clicking.
The PyMOL Interface — What You're Looking At
  1. Viewer (centre): the 3D viewport. Left-click + drag to rotate. Right-click + drag to zoom. Middle-click + drag to translate.
  2. Object panel (right): lists all loaded structures/objects. Each has buttons: A (actions) · S (show) · H (hide) · L (label) · C (colour)
  3. Command line (bottom): type commands here. Everything you can do by clicking can also be done by command — and commands are faster once you know them.
  4. Sequence bar (top): toggle with Display → Sequence. Click residues here to select them.
Loading a Structure
  1. Drag and drop a .pdb file onto the PyMOL window — simplest method
  2. Or fetch directly from PDB: type in the command line:
    fetch 6HAX
    This downloads the structure automatically — you need internet connection.
  3. Or: File → Open → navigate to your .pdb file
Essential Commands (type in the command line)
CommandWhat it doesWhy you'd use it
hide everythingHides all representationsStart fresh before applying your own style
show cartoonShows protein backbone as ribbonsStandard view for seeing secondary structure (helices, sheets)
show sticks, organicShows small molecules as sticksSee your ligand in the binding pocket
show surfaceMolecular surface around the whole proteinSee the shape of the binding pocket from the outside
color white, allColours everything whiteNeutral starting point for figures
color cyan, chain AColours chain A cyanDistinguish chains in a complex (use different colours per chain)
color yellow, organicColours ligands yellowMake ligands stand out against protein
zoom organicCentres view on the ligandJump straight to the binding site
select binding_site, byres (organic expand 5)Selects all residues within 5 Å of the ligandIdentify binding site residues quickly
show sticks, binding_siteShows those residues as sticksSee the side chains interacting with your ligand
distance d1, resi 106 and name OG, organic and name O3Draws a distance measurementMeasure H-bond or interaction distances
bg_color whiteWhite backgroundNeeded for publication figures
set ray_shadows, 0Turns off ray-tracing shadowsCleaner look for figures
ray 2400, 1800Renders high-res image (2400×1800 px)Save a publication-quality image
png figure1.png, dpi=300Saves the rendered imageExport for paper/presentation
Useful Selections
PyMOL uses a selection language. The key words are: chain, resi (residue number), resn (residue name), name (atom name), organic (all small molecules), polymer (protein/DNA), solvent (water). Combine with and, or, not.
SelectionWhat it selects
chain AEverything in chain A
resi 50-100Residues 50 to 100
resn LIGA specific ligand by its 3-letter code (find the code on the PDB page)
organicAll non-protein, non-water molecules (ligands, cofactors)
byres (organic expand 4)All residues with any atom within 4 Å of any ligand atom
not solvent and not organicProtein only, no water or ligand
Aligning Two Structures
  1. Load both structures (e.g. fetch 6HAX and fetch 4TZ4)
  2. Align one onto the other:
    align 4TZ4, 6HAX
    This superimposes 4TZ4 onto 6HAX based on Cα positions. PyMOL reports RMSD — lower = more similar.
  3. Use super instead of align for better results when structures diverge significantly
Pip Tip
When you first open a PDB file, PyMOL often shows a messy default view with waters, ions, and multiple chains. A clean starting point for almost any analysis:
hide everything
remove solvent
show cartoon, polymer
show sticks, organic
color grey80, polymer
color yellow, organic
zoom organic This hides water, shows the protein as a clean cartoon, highlights your ligand in yellow, and centres on the binding site.
PyMOL — Publication-Quality Figures PyMOL ▶
Why a specific workflow for figures? Default PyMOL screenshots look flat and grey. Good structural figures for a paper need a white background, good colours, clear representation of the binding site, and high resolution. This workflow takes ~15 min once you know it.
