Standard Figure Setup
  1. Set white background: bg_color white
  2. Remove clutter: remove solvent · hide everything
  3. Protein cartoon, coloured by secondary structure or chain:
    show cartoon, polymer then color slate, polymer (nice blue-grey)
  4. Ligand as sticks, coloured by element:
    show sticks, organic · util.cbay organic (carbon=yellow, heteroatoms by element)
  5. Show binding site residues:
    select bsite, byres (organic expand 4)
    show sticks, bsite · util.cbaw bsite (carbon=white for contrast with ligand)
  6. Add transparent surface over pocket:
    show surface, polymer · set transparency, 0.7
  7. Turn off shadows and set ray trace mode:
    set ray_shadows, 0 · set ray_trace_mode, 1
  8. Orient your view, then render:
    ray 2400, 1800 then png figure1.png, dpi=300
Useful Colour Schemes
CommandResultGood for
color slateBlue-greyClean protein cartoon
color grey80Light greyProtein when ligand is the focus
util.cbayYellow carbons, colour by elementLigand of interest
util.cbawWhite carbons, colour by elementBinding site residues (contrast vs ligand)
util.cbacCyan carbonsSecond ligand or second chain
spectrum b, blue_white_redB-factor mapped coloursHighlight flexible regions
spectrum count, rainbowRainbow N→C terminusShow chain direction
H-bonds and Interactions
  1. Show H-bonds between ligand and protein:
    distance hbonds, organic, byres (organic expand 3.5), mode=2
    This draws dashed lines for potential H-bond donors/acceptors within 3.5 Å
  2. Label a residue: label resi 106 and name CA, "%s%s" % (resn, resi)
  3. Hide distance labels if too cluttered: hide labels
Pip Tip
Save your PyMOL session (.pse file) before rendering — this preserves all your selections, colours and orientations. In papers, it's good practice to note the PDB code, any chains removed, and the software version used in the Methods section.
Binding Site & Pocket Analysis CASTp · FPocket · ChimeraX ▶
Why identify pockets? To design a ligand, you need to know where to put it. If a co-crystal structure with a known ligand exists, the pocket is obvious — just look at where the ligand sits. But for apo structures (no ligand) or novel proteins, you need computational tools to identify cavities large enough and shaped correctly to bind a drug-like molecule.
Case 1 — Structure Has a Known Ligand (easiest)
  1. Load structure in PyMOL, zoom to ligand: zoom organic
  2. The cavity around the ligand is your binding pocket. Select residues within 4–5 Å: select pocket, byres (organic expand 4)
  3. Show surface of pocket to see its shape: show surface, pocket
  4. Identify hydrophobic vs polar residues — relevant for ligand design (check residue identities in sequence bar)
Case 2 — Apo Structure or No Ligand (use CASTp)
  1. Go to CASTp server — upload your .pdb file
  2. Set probe radius to 1.4 Å (standard, size of a water molecule)
  3. Submit — results come back within minutes as a list of pockets ranked by volume (Å³)
  4. Download the annotated PDB and load in PyMOL to visualise which pocket is which
  5. Larger volume + concave shape + hydrophobic character = more druggable pocket
Druggability Assessment
Not all pockets are druggable. A pocket needs to be: (1) large enough (~300–1000 Å³ for a drug-sized molecule), (2) sufficiently enclosed / concave (not a flat surface), (3) has a mix of hydrophobic and H-bonding residues to engage a ligand. Protein–protein interaction (PPI) interfaces are notoriously hard — flat and large. PPI inhibitors (like BET bromodomain inhibitors) work because the interface has a hot-spot cavity.
Comparing Pockets Between Structures (Conformational Changes)
  1. Load apo and holo (ligand-bound) structures; align them: align structure_apo, structure_holo
  2. Look for induced-fit changes: loop movements, helix shifts around the binding site
  3. This is relevant for understanding whether your target has an induced-fit mechanism (relevant for fragment-based design)
Pip Tip
For PROTAC targets, an interesting question is not just "where does my POI ligand bind?" but also "is there a surface patch on my POI that is close to the E3 ligase surface in a productive ternary complex?". This is the neo-substrate interface concept. Structures of CRBN or VHL with their known degraders give you a template for where the linker exits — use these as a guide when thinking about linker vectors on your own target.
Ternary Complex Modelling (PROTAC / Degrader) AF3 · HDOCK · PyMOL ▶
What is a ternary complex? A PROTAC simultaneously binds both your target protein (POI) and an E3 ubiquitin ligase (e.g. CRBN or VHL), bringing them into proximity so the E3 can ubiquitinate the POI. The ternary complex (POI–PROTAC–E3) is the active species for degradation. Understanding its geometry helps explain why some PROTACs work and others don't, and guides linker design.
Why Model It?
  1. Ternary complex formation is cooperative — a good PROTAC stabilises a specific POI:E3 interface beyond what the two proteins alone would form
  2. The geometry of the complex (which POI surface faces the E3, distance to ubiquitination site) determines degradation efficiency
  3. Models help you identify: which linker exit vectors are productive · whether linker length is appropriate · whether there are clashes at the interface
Step 1 — Gather Your Starting Structures
  1. POI with its ligand bound (from PDB or docked)
  2. E3 ligase with its ligand bound — for CRBN: PDB 4CI3 (thalidomide) or search for your specific warhead; for VHL: PDB 4B9K (VHL-HIF peptide)
  3. If no co-crystal: dock your ligands first using Glide (Schrödinger) or Gnina (free)
Step 2 — Predict the Ternary Complex
  1. AlphaFold3 (easiest, free): go to alphafoldserver.com → Add sequences for both POI and E3 ligase → optionally add your PROTAC SMILES as a ligand → Submit. Download the .cif result.
  2. HDOCK (protein–protein docking, free): go to hdock.phys.hust.edu.cn → upload both proteins → optionally restrain interface residues if you have HDX or mutagenesis data → Submit. Download top models.
  3. ClusPro (alternative, free): cluspro.bu.edu — similar workflow, sometimes better for large complexes
Step 3 — Evaluate the Model in PyMOL
  1. Load the complex: drag .pdb/.cif onto PyMOL
  2. Colour each chain differently: color slate, chain A · color salmon, chain B
  3. Find the ligands: show sticks, organic · color yellow, organic
  4. Measure distance between the two ligand exit vectors (where the linker would attach): distance linker_dist, resi X and name CX, resi Y and name CY
  5. Check for clashes at the interface: select interface, byres (chain A within 5 of chain B) then show sticks, interface
  6. Check that lysines on the POI surface near the E3 are accessible: Lys residues within ubiquitination range (~40–50 Å from E2 active site Cys) are productive
Step 4 — Linker Length Estimation
A rough rule: each PEG unit (~3.8 Å) or each carbon in an alkyl chain (~1.25 Å extended) contributes to the linker length. Measure the distance between the two exit vectors in your model and use this to estimate minimum linker length needed. In practice, linkers 4–14 atoms long are most common in active PROTACs, but the geometry of your specific complex matters more than a rule of thumb.
Pip Tip
The Ciulli lab has published extensively on ternary complex structures and cooperativity. Notable resources: the 2019 Nature Chemical Biology paper on PROTAC cooperativity and the PDB 6BN7 (SMARCA2 BRD–ACBI1–VHL ternary complex crystal structure) are excellent reference points for what a productive ternary complex looks like structurally. Worth loading 6BN7 in PyMOL as a learning exercise before tackling your own target.
Assays
Echo · TR-FRET · Fluorescence Polarization · SPR
Assay Binding · Affinity · Kinetics
TR-FRET — Ternary Complex Formation Assay — ▶
What is this? Time-Resolved FRET (TR-FRET) uses a long-lifetime lanthanide donor (typically Terbium, Tb) and a short-lifetime acceptor (e.g. Alexa Fluor 488, d2, or BODIPY). By gating the measurement after a delay (>50 µs), background autofluorescence is eliminated. FRET only occurs when donor and acceptor are within ~10 nm, i.e. when a ternary complex (POI–PROTAC–E3 ligase) forms. In the PROTAC context, TR-FRET is the gold-standard biochemical assay to detect and quantify ternary complex formation, determine the maximal efficacy concentration (Cmax), and characterise cooperativity (α) — all in a homogeneous, no-wash format suitable for HTS in 384-well plates.
When to use it: Early-to-mid PROTAC campaign — comparing a series of linker lengths or exit vectors, determining the bell-shaped dose-response and hook effect, measuring cooperativity vs. FP/SPR. Requires purified recombinant proteins with orthogonal tags (e.g. GST on POI, His on CRBN-DDB1). CoraFluor-1 (Tb cryptate) anti-His nanobody plus GST-antibody labelled with Lumi4-Tb is the Ciulli lab preferred reagent set (Payne & Mazitschek 2022).
Principle & Key Parameters
  1. Donor: Tb-labelled antibody or nanobody against tag on E3 ligase subunit (e.g. Tb-anti-His against His6-CRBN-DDB1). Emission measured at 490 nm (Tb) and 520 nm (acceptor). Ratio = 10,000 × (520 nm / 490 nm) = ratiometric TR-FRET unit (RTU).
  2. Acceptor: fluorophore-labelled antibody against tag on POI (e.g. AF488-anti-GST against GST-BRD4). Must have good spectral overlap with Tb emission.
  3. Bell-shaped curve: at low [PROTAC], ternary complex increases (rising phase); at high [PROTAC], binary complexes dominate and displace ternary (hook effect / falling phase). The peak defines Cmax and EC50,ternary.
  4. Cooperativity α: α = KD,binary / KD,ternary. α > 1 = positive cooperativity (PROTAC binds E3 tighter when POI is already bound). Determine by comparing FP IC50 with and without saturating POI.
  5. DMSO tolerance: typically ≤1% DMSO final. Test DMSO titration before full compound screen — TR-FRET signal should be flat up to 1% and drop only above 2%.
Reagents & Setup
  1. Buffer: 25 mM HEPES pH 7.4, 150 mM NaCl, 0.5 mM TCEP, 0.05% Tween-20, 0.1% BSA. Prepare fresh; Tween-20 and BSA critical to prevent aggregation and non-specific binding.
  2. Purified proteins: E3 ligase with orthogonal tag (e.g. His6-CRBN-DDB1), POI with orthogonal tag (e.g. GST-BRD4(BD1)). Check purity ≥90% by SDS-PAGE/Coomassie. Flash-freeze aliquots; avoid repeated freeze-thaw.
  3. Detection reagents: Tb-labelled antibody/nanobody against E3 tag (donor); fluorophore-labelled antibody against POI tag (acceptor). Optimise concentration via matrix titration (see below).
  4. Plates: white 384-well low-volume plates (e.g. Greiner 784075). White walls maximise signal. 10–20 µL total reaction volume.
  5. Compound: prepare 10–11 point dose-response (typically 10 µM – 0.1 nM, 3-fold dilutions) in 100% DMSO, then dilute 1:100 into assay to achieve 1% DMSO final. Include DMSO control and positive control (known PROTAC at Cmax).
Optimisation (Stepwise, after Sakamoto et al. ACS PTSI 2021)
  1. Step 1 — Antibody titration: fix POI and E3 at low nM; titrate donor Ab (0.25–8 nM) and acceptor Ab (0.5–16 nM) in a matrix. Identify conditions where DMSO-only baseline is low and PROTAC-induced signal is maximal (≥2-fold over DMSO). Aim: Tb-anti-His ~2 nM, AF488-anti-GST ~2–4 nM.
  2. Step 2 — Protein titration: fix optimal antibody concentrations. Titrate POI and E3 (1–20 nM each). Rule of thumb: use proteins at or slightly below KD of PROTAC to each protein. For CRBN-based PROTACs: GST-BRD4 ~2 nM, His-CRBN-DDB1 ~8 nM (4:1 ratio) is a well-validated starting point from the literature.
  3. Step 3 — Incubation time: measure signal at 30, 60, 120, 180, 300 min. TR-FRET must reach equilibrium. Typically 120–180 min at RT in the dark sufficient for most PROTACs. If signal continues to rise at 180 min, extend to 240 min.
  4. Step 4 — DMSO tolerance: add 0, 0.5, 1, 2, 4% DMSO. Assay should be stable at ≤1% DMSO. Normalise all data as fold-change to DMSO control.
  5. Step 5 — PROTAC dose-response: run full 10-point curve under optimised conditions. Fit to bell-shaped model or report EC50 and EC50,hook separately. Report signal as fold-change to DMSO.
Protocol — 384-well setup (20 uL)
Reagent system: Alexa Fluor 647-labelled Protein A (donor on POI), biotinylated Protein B (E3 ligase subunit), LANCE Streptavidin-Europium (PerkinElmer #AD0062) as the lanthanide donor bridged via biotin. This LANCE-based format uses Eu chelate (excitation 337 nm) with AF647 as acceptor (emission 665 nm), and a short-wavelength reference channel at 620 nm for ratiometric correction.
  1. Prepare assay buffer: 25 mM HEPES pH 7.5, 50 mM NaCl, 2 mM TCEP, 0.005% Tween-20. Prepare fresh; TCEP critical for cysteine-containing proteins.
  2. Prepare assay solution with the following final concentrations: Alexa647-labelled Protein A 25 nM, biotinylated Protein B 2.5 nM, LANCE Streptavidin Fluorophore (PerkinElmer #AD0062) 0.25 nM. Mix components in assay buffer in this order; allow Streptavidin-biotin interaction to form before adding compound.
  3. Dispense compounds into Corning 384-well black plates (black walls reduce optical crosstalk). Echo Acoustic Dispenser recommended for DMSO compound stocks.
  4. Add assay solution to each well: 20 µL/well final volume. Seal plate with foil.
  5. Incubate at room temperature for 17 hours (overnight). This extended incubation ensures equilibrium is reached for slow-forming ternary complexes — critical for accurate EC50 determination.
  6. Read on PHERAstar FS (BMG LABTECH): excitation 337 nm, dual emission 620 nm (Eu reference) and 665 nm (AF647 FRET signal). Integration time 70–400 µs.
  7. Calculate TR-FRET ratio = 665 nm / 620 nm (× 10,000 for RTU). Normalise to DMSO control. Fit dose-response in GraphPad Prism v10 (bell-shaped or 4PL model depending on hook effect).
Pip Tip
Pip Tip — Ciulli lab: CoraFluor-1 (Tb cryptate from Payne & Mazitschek, 2022) coupled to Nano-Secondaries (single-domain anti-species nanobodies) offers superior performance vs. direct antibody labelling — eliminates batch-to-batch variability and works well in lysate-based assays. For binary target engagement (KD determination), use the competitive displacement format: pre-form binary complex at KD concentration, then titrate competing compound. This is more robust than direct binding for small molecules. See Payne et al. (2021) JACS for full CoraFluor characterisation.
Fluorescence Polarization (FP) — Binary Binding & Cooperativity — ▶
What is this? Fluorescence polarization measures the rotational freedom of a small fluorescent molecule (tracer). When free in solution, a small fluorescent tracer tumbles rapidly → low polarization (mP). When bound to a large protein, rotation slows dramatically → high polarization. In a competitive FP assay, an unlabelled compound displaces the tracer from the protein, reducing polarization in a concentration-dependent manner, enabling IC50 and KD determination.
When to use it: FP is the workhorse assay for binary KD determination of PROTAC warheads to their E3 ligase (VHL or CRBN). It is fast, cheap, requires no specialist instrumentation, and works with standard fluorescence plate readers. For VHL: FAM-labelled HIF-1α peptide tracer + VCB complex. For CRBN: Cy5- or TAMRA-labelled thalidomide derivative + CRBN-DDB1. Also used to measure cooperativity α by comparing IC50 of the PROTAC against E3 with and without saturating concentrations of the POI.
Principle
  1. Excite tracer with plane-polarized light; measure emission in parallel (I||) and perpendicular (I⊥) planes.
  2. Polarization mP = 1000 × (I|| − I⊥) / (I|| + I⊥). Bound state ~250–350 mP; free tracer ~20–50 mP. Dynamic range = mPbound − mPfree ≥ 150 mP preferred.
  3. Competition: titrate compound against pre-formed tracer–protein complex. IC50 converted to KD via Cheng–Prusoff: KD = IC50 / (1 + [tracer] / KD,tracer).
  4. Cooperativity α: run FP competition assay in parallel with and without saturating POI (e.g. BRD4 at ≥10× KD,PROTAC–BRD4). α = IC50,without POI / IC50,with POI. α > 1 = positive cooperativity.
VHL FP Assay — Reagents
  1. Tracer: FAM-labelled HIF-1α peptide (e.g. FAM-DLDLEMLAPYIPMDDDFQL or similar hydroxyproline-containing peptide). [Tracer] = 10–20 nM (at or below KD,tracer ~5–10 nM to maintain sensitivity).
  2. Protein: VCB complex (VHL:ElonginC:ElonginB, His-tagged). Typical assay concentration: ~100–200 nM (near KD,tracer or slightly above to ensure ≥80% tracer bound at start).
  3. Buffer: 25 mM HEPES pH 7.5, 150 mM NaCl, 0.5 mM TCEP, 0.01% Tween-20. Tween-20 prevents well-surface sticking. No BSA (can interfere with FP).
  4. Plates: black flat-bottom 96-well or 384-well plates (Corning 3575 or equivalent). Black walls minimise background fluorescence. 20–50 µL reaction volume.
VHL FP Assay Protocol
  1. Prepare compound serial dilutions in assay buffer (8–11 points, 3-fold dilutions, top concentration 100–500 µM). Maximum 1% DMSO final. Run in duplicate or triplicate.
  2. Prepare protein-tracer mix: VCB at 2× final concentration + FAM-tracer at 2× final concentration in assay buffer. Pre-incubate 30 min on ice to allow equilibration.
  3. Add 10 µL compound to wells; add 10 µL protein-tracer mix. Total 20 µL.
  4. Incubate 30–60 min at RT in the dark.
  5. Read FP on suitable reader (PHERAstar, EnVision, BioTek): excitation 485 nm, emission 535 nm. Report in mP.
  6. Controls: tracer only (min FP, ~20–50 mP), tracer + protein (max FP, ~250–350 mP). Normalise data: % inhibition = (mPsample − mPmin) / (mPmax − mPmin) × 100. Fit to sigmoidal dose-response for IC50.
  7. For KD: if [protein] ≥10× KD, use direct binding fit. Otherwise, use Cheng-Prusoff correction.
CRBN FP Assay — Notes
  1. Tracer: Cy5- or TAMRA-labelled thalidomide, pomalidomide or lenalidomide derivative (several commercially available from Tocris, MedChemExpress, or custom synthesis). [Tracer] = 10–50 nM.
  2. Protein: His-CRBN-DDB1 complex (requires co-expression). Use 200–500 nM. CRBN-DDB1 is large (~170 kDa), giving a good signal window. Avoid CRBN-TBD alone — lower MW gives smaller dynamic range.
  3. IMiD positive controls: pomalidomide (KD ~1–5 µM), lenalidomide (~10–50 µM) displace tracer. Validate rank order with literature before running compounds.
  4. Interfering compounds: PROTAC scaffolds can be fluorescent (especially with extended aromatic systems). Check compound at highest concentration for fluorescence at FAM/Cy5 excitation before running assay. Inner filter effects are the most common artefact.
Cooperativity by FP
  1. Run standard FP competition assay for PROTAC vs. E3 ligase tracer.
  2. In parallel, add saturating POI (e.g. BRD4(BD1) at 10× KD,PROTAC-BRD4) to all wells; repeat FP competition.
  3. If ternary complex is cooperative, IC50 will shift left (lower IC50 → apparent tighter binding to E3). α = IC50,binary / IC50,ternary.
  4. Negative cooperativity: IC50 shifts right. Additive: IC50 unchanged.
Pip Tip
FP is exquisitely sensitive to inner filter effects with coloured compounds — always measure compound absorbance at the excitation/emission wavelengths and include a reference well. Large PROTACs (>800 Da) can sometimes shift FP even when not specifically competing (non-specific viscosity/quenching effects). Validate hits with an orthogonal method (ITC, SPR) before concluding on KD. The FAM-HIF1α/VCB FP assay is extremely well validated in the Ciulli lab — benchmark your VHL warheads against VH032 (KD ~100 nM) and VH298 (KD ~80 nM) as positive controls.
SPR (Biacore) — Binary & Ternary Complex Kinetics — ▶
What is this? Surface Plasmon Resonance (SPR) is a label-free, real-time optical technique that detects changes in refractive index near a sensor chip surface. One protein is immobilised on the chip; binding partner flows over it and the instrument records association and dissociation in real time as a sensorgram (RU vs. time). SPR provides kinetic rate constants (kon, koff) and equilibrium affinity (KD = koff/kon) directly, without labels. For PROTACs, SPR enables the only direct measurement of ternary complex dissociation kinetics (koff,ternary) — a parameter that correlates with degradation rate. Pioneered for PROTACs by Roy, Winkler, Ciulli et al. (ACS Chem. Biol. 2019).
When to use it: Mid-to-late campaign, when FP/TR-FRET have identified promising compounds and you need kinetic insight — especially when comparing compounds with similar Cmax but different cellular potency. Also essential for establishing structure-activity relationships for cooperativity. Requires Biacore instrument (T200, 8K, or 8K+), sufficient purified protein (>200 µg of E3 ligase for immobilisation), and careful surface chemistry.
SPR Assay Design — Ternary Complex
  1. Immobilised protein (ligand): E3 ligase component on chip. For VHL-based PROTACs: biotinylated VCB captured on Streptavidin chip (SA chip, Cytiva). Density: target 200–500 RU to minimise avidity effects and mass transfer limitations. Biotinylate using BirA enzyme and AviTag; validate by BCA and SDS-PAGE.
  2. Flowing analyte (binary): PROTAC alone (typically 0.1–10× KD, 6–8 concentrations, 3-fold dilutions). Use multi-cycle kinetics (MCK) for high-affinity binders (KD < 100 nM, slow koff). Use single-cycle kinetics (SCK) for fast-dissociating species.
  3. Flowing analyte (ternary): pre-form PROTAC:POI binary complex by pre-incubating PROTAC with saturating POI (e.g. 10 µM BRD4(BD1) with 1 µM PROTAC, 30 min on ice). This pre-formed complex is then flowed over the E3 chip. The resulting binding curve reports on the ternary complex kinetics.
  4. Cooperativity α by SPR: α = KD,binary / KD,ternary. If α > 1, the PROTAC:POI complex binds VHL more tightly than PROTAC alone → positive cooperativity. α < 1 = negative cooperativity. α = 1 = additive.
Running Buffer & Surface Prep
  1. Running buffer: PBS-P+ (Cytiva) or 10 mM HEPES pH 7.4, 150 mM NaCl, 0.005% Tween-20, 0.5 mM TCEP. Degas and filter (0.22 µm) before use. For small molecules: add 1–2% DMSO if needed (must calibrate DMSO correction curve).
  2. Reference surface: blank-captured or reference channel without protein. Used for double-referencing (subtract reference channel + blank injection).
  3. DMSO correction: if compounds in DMSO, perform DMSO calibration (0.5–3% DMSO 7-point curve) and apply solvent correction. Critical — without this, DMSO bulk refractive index causes false signal.
  4. Surface regeneration: for SA-captured biotinylated protein, typically no regeneration needed. If POI:PROTAC:E3 ternary complex dissociates slowly, short 10 mM glycine pH 1.5 pulse (5 s) or 1 M NaCl pulse restores surface; validate that regeneration does not reduce capacity.
Protocol — Binary Binding (PROTAC vs. immobilised VCB)
  1. Prime system with running buffer; condition chip with 3× buffer injections.
  2. Capture biotinylated VCB on SA chip: inject 10–50 nM VCB-biotin at 5 µL/min until desired RU reached (~300 RU). Block remaining SA sites with 3× 1 min biotin injections.
  3. Prepare PROTAC dilution series in running buffer (1% DMSO final). Typical: 1000, 333, 111, 37, 12, 4, 1.3 nM (7 points, 3-fold). Include blank injections (buffer only) for double-referencing.
  4. Inject each concentration: association 60–120 s, dissociation 120–300 s, flow rate 30–100 µL/min. Double-reference all data.
  5. Fit to 1:1 Langmuir model in Biacore Evaluation Software or Scrubber2. Extract kon, koff, KD.
Protocol — Ternary Complex (Pre-formed PROTAC:POI vs. VCB)
  1. Pre-incubate PROTAC with saturating POI (e.g. 10 µM BRD4(BD1) + titrated PROTAC concentrations: 1000, 333, 111, 37, 12 nM) in running buffer, 30 min on ice.
  2. Use single-cycle kinetics format: inject concentrations sequentially without regeneration (association 100 s per concentration, final dissociation 600–1200 s). This minimises surface stress.
  3. Include control: POI alone (same concentrations, no PROTAC) to confirm POI does not bind E3 in the absence of PROTAC.
  4. Double-reference and fit to 1:1 model. The ternary KD and koff,ternary are key outputs.
  5. Calculate α = KD,binary (PROTAC alone vs. VCB) / KD,ternary (PROTAC:POI vs. VCB).
Interpreting Results in the Degrader Context
  1. koff,ternary predicts degradation rate: slower dissociation = longer ternary complex half-life = more time for E2 recruitment and ubiquitin transfer. MZ1 (BRD4BD2/VHL, α ~15–26) has t1/2,off ~60–90 s; this correlates with faster and more complete BRD4 degradation vs. equipotent analogues with shorter ternary complex lifetimes.
  2. Hook effect visible in SPR: at very high [PROTAC], binary complexes dominate and less pre-formed PROTAC:POI binary complex is available → apparent RU drops at highest concentrations. Confirm this is hook effect (not artefact) by checking the free PROTAC binding to E3 at same concentrations.
  3. Cooperativity α > 50: rare but powerful; seen for extremely well-packed ternary complexes with extensive neo-PPI surface. Confirms that linker/exit vector design is stabilising PPI contacts. Target α ≥ 2–5 as a minimum for productive degradation; α > 10 for high-cooperativity compounds.
  4. Negative cooperativity (α < 1): PROTAC:POI binary complex binds E3 less well than PROTAC alone → POI disfavours ternary complex. Consider alternative exit vector from POI, shorter/longer linker, or different E3 ligase. Not always fatal for degradation (some good degraders have α < 1) but worth addressing in SAR.
Pip Tip
Pip Tip — from the lab: The Ciulli lab protocol uses biotin-VCB immobilised on SA chip, multi-cycle kinetics for binary PROTAC binding, and single-cycle kinetics for ternary complex to avoid slow-off artefacts during regeneration. TCEP in buffer is critical for VHL (disulphide-sensitive). For CRBN-DDB1-based ternary SPR: His6-CRBN-DDB1 can be captured via anti-His antibody surface (Cytiva His Capture Kit) avoiding the need to chemically biotinylate — more convenient for multiple batches. Note that some BRD4(BD1) PROTAC ternary complexes with VHL dissociate faster than the instrument’s dead time (>1 s−1); single-cycle kinetics with longer association windows partially mitigates this. Roy et al. 2019 ACS Chem. Biol. and the Ciulli lab Methods chapter (Springer Nature Protocols 2021) are the definitive references for this assay format.
PROTAC Tools
Ligase Dependency Index · Dmax Normalizer
Ligase Dependency Index (LDI)
LDI = (DC₅₀CRBN KO − DC₅₀VHL KO) / (DC₅₀CRBN KO + DC₅₀VHL KO) ·  −1 = VHL  ·  0 = Dual  ·  +1 = CRBN
Columns: Compound, WT, CRBN_KO, VHL_KO, DKO in uM  ·  Censored/inactive: >value (e.g. >10)  ·  DKO flag: active in double-KO → possible non-CRBN/VHL mechanism
Compound WT DC₅₀ (uM) CRBN KO DC₅₀ (uM) VHL KO DC₅₀ (uM) DKO DC₅₀ (uM)
Classification: VHL-dep. Dual / mixed CRBN-dep. N/A DKO active = non-CRBN/VHL mechanism possible
Results
LDI by Compound
DC₅₀ CRBN KO vs VHL KO (log scale)
Edit scatter plot…
Axis ranges in uM. When unit is M, you can enter actual values (1e-9) or log₁₀ (-9).
Dmax Normalizer
What is this? Rescales dose–response data to correct the Bottom plateau (Dmax) to a target value, while leaving the Top plateau exactly as observed. DC₅₀ is preserved. Apply the output formula to each raw data point before re-fitting in Prism.
Observed Top
Observed Bottom
Target Bottom
Scale factor m
—
Formula — apply to each raw data point
Verification
Input xTransformed yExpected
x = Top observed—= Top observed (unchanged)
x = Bottom observed—= Target bottom
x = 0.50—intermediate point
How to apply in GraphPad Prism
  1. Open your data table in Prism
  2. Go to Analyze → Transform
  3. Select User-defined transform
  4. Enter: Y = MAX(0, M*Y + B) replacing M and B with the values above
  5. Click OK — Prism creates a new transformed table and re-fits all curves automatically
Pip Tip
DC₅₀ is unaffected — linear rescaling in Y preserves the inflection point on the x-axis. The Top plateau is left exactly as observed, so curves that are already near 1.0 stay near 1.0. Only the Bottom (Dmax) is corrected. Typical use: Target Bottom = 0.01 (Dmax 99%). Useful for comparing Dmax across experiments where top plateaux are already well-anchored.

Pip Tip
Dissolve components in ~80% of the target volume, adjust pH, then top up to final volume. Always add protease inhibitors fresh to lysis buffers.

SDS-PAGE, fluorochromes & amino acids
SDS-PAGE Acrylamide Guide
% GelSeparation RangeBest For
6%50 – 400 kDaVery large proteins (myosin, titin)
8%30 – 200 kDaLarge proteins (IgG ~150 kDa)
10%20 – 120 kDaMedium proteins — most common choice
12%10 – 70 kDaSmall-medium proteins
15%3 – 40 kDaSmall proteins, peptides
4–15% grad.10 – 200 kDaBroad range, good for unknowns
4–20% grad.10 – 250 kDaWidest range, multiple targets
For stacking gels use 5% acrylamide. Running buffer: 25 mM Tris, 192 mM glycine, 0.1% SDS, pH 8.3. Standard: 200 V, 45–60 min.

Cell Lines
Culture conditions & handling notes — click a tile to open
Condensed handling information from ATCC product sheets. Always confirm against the official ATCC page for the exact lot/vial, and follow the lab's mycoplasma/STR policy before any assay.
🧫 22Rv1 Prostate carcinoma · ATCC CRL-2505 ATCC page
Phase contrast · © ATCC
Organism / tissueHuman · prostate; androgen-independent xenograft derivative (CWR22 lineage)
MorphologyEpithelial, adherent
Complete mediumRPMI-1640 (ATCC 30-2001) + 10% FBS
Atmosphere37 °C · 95% air / 5% CO2
Doubling time~37–40 h
Subculture0.25% trypsin-EDTA · split 1:3 to 1:6 · 2×/week
Key featuresAR-FL + AR-V7 splice variant (ligand-independent), TMPRSS2-ERG neg, p53 WT
Pip Tip: The key castration-resistant prostate cancer model and the go-to line for showing that a PROTAC degrades both full-length AR and the antiandrogen-resistant AR-V7 splice variant (enzalutamide/apalutamide are inactive on V7). Much better behaved than LNCaP at the bench — attaches properly and tolerates trypsin — but cells do like to clump, so a short, gentle trituration before plating gives more uniform seeding. Great companion to LNCaP + VCaP in a 3-line AR-degrader panel.
🧫 A549 Lung carcinoma · ATCC CCL-185 ATCC page
Phase contrast · © ATCC
Organism / tissueHuman · lung; non-small cell lung adenocarcinoma (alveolar type II)
MorphologyEpithelial, adherent
Complete mediumF-12K (ATCC 30-2004) + 10% FBS
Atmosphere37 °C · 95% air / 5% CO2
Doubling time~22 h (fast)
Subculture0.25% trypsin-EDTA · split 1:3 to 1:8 · 2×/week
Key featuresKRASG12S, KEAP1 mutant (NRF2-high), CDKN2A deleted, p53 WT
Pip Tip: Tough, fast, and tolerant — A549 is a good "sanity check" NSCLC line for CETSA, NanoBRET and cytotoxicity workflows. The KEAP1 loss / NRF2 activation phenotype is clinically and mechanistically important: expect high basal antioxidant capacity, which blunts oxidative-stress readouts and can sideline some Michael-acceptor warheads. Note on medium: ATCC ships A549 in F-12K; many labs maintain it in DMEM or DMEM/F-12 without issue, but stay consistent within a study.
🧫 HCT116 Colorectal carcinoma · ATCC CCL-247 ATCC page
Phase contrast · © ATCC
Organism / tissueHuman · colon, adult male; colorectal carcinoma
MorphologyEpithelial, adherent
Complete mediumMcCoy's 5a Modified (ATCC 30-2007) + 10% FBS
Atmosphere37 °C · 95% air / 5% CO2
Doubling time~17–21 h (fast)
Subculture0.25% trypsin-EDTA · split 1:3 to 1:8 · 2–3×/week
Key featuresKRASG13D, PIK3CAH1047R, MSI-high, p53 wild-type, CTNNB1 mutant
Pip Tip: Fast, forgiving, and genetically well-characterised — a good "default" colon line for PROTAC / degrader viability & target engagement studies. Because it's p53 WT, it's a clean background to see apoptotic readouts after MDM2 or target-degrader treatment. The isogenic HCT116 p53−/− (Vogelstein) line is a useful paired control if you're dissecting p53-dependent effects.
🧫 HEK293 Human embryonic kidney · ATCC CRL-1573 ATCC page
Phase contrast · © ATCC
Organism / tissueHuman · kidney (embryonic); transformed with sheared Ad5 DNA
MorphologyEpithelial-like, adherent — but loosely attached
Complete mediumEMEM (ATCC 30-2003) + 10% FBS
Atmosphere37 °C · 95% air / 5% CO2
Doubling time~24–36 h
Subculture0.25% trypsin-EDTA · split 1:3 to 1:8 · 2×/week
StorageBelow −130 °C (LN2 vapour) — freeze medium: complete medium + 5% DMSO
Pip Tip: Cells detach very easily — a firm tap on the flask is often enough and over-trypsinising kills them. For virus production / transient overexpression you almost certainly want HEK293T (SV40 Large-T variant, ATCC CRL-3216) rather than parental 293. Despite the name, recent lineage work suggests the line is actually of neuronal origin, not kidney epithelium — worth remembering when using it as a "generic" cell background for degrader work.
🧫 LNCaP (clone FGC) Prostate carcinoma · ATCC CRL-1740 ATCC page
Phase contrast · © ATCC
Organism / tissueHuman · prostate, lymph node metastasis
MorphologyEpithelial, adherent — very loosely attached
Complete mediumRPMI-1640 (ATCC 30-2001) + 10% FBS
Atmosphere37 °C · 95% air / 5% CO2
Doubling time~28–60 h (slow, variable)
Subculture0.25% trypsin-EDTA (brief) · split 1:3 to 1:6 · 1–2×/week
Key featuresART878A (androgen-responsive but promiscuous), PTEN-null, p53 WT
Pip Tip: The workhorse AR+ line for AR-degrader / AR-PROTAC work. Handle gently — even medium changes can dislodge cells; pre-warm media, pipette slowly down the wall of the flask, and consider collagen- or poly-D-lysine-coated plates for assays needing even monolayers. The T878A mutation makes AR promiscuous (responds to progestins, oestrogens, hydroxyflutamide), so interpret agonist/antagonist SAR with caution. For androgen-free assays use phenol-red-free RPMI + charcoal-stripped FBS.
🧫 MCF7 Breast adenocarcinoma · ATCC HTB-22 ATCC page
Phase contrast · © ATCC
Organism / tissueHuman · mammary gland; pleural effusion (metastatic)
MorphologyEpithelial, adherent — forms domes at confluence
Complete mediumEMEM (ATCC 30-2003) + 10% FBS + 0.01 mg/mL human recombinant insulin
Atmosphere37 °C · 95% air / 5% CO2
Doubling time~29–40 h
Subculture0.25% trypsin-EDTA · split 1:3 to 1:6 · 2×/week
Key featuresER+, PR+, HER2−; PIK3CAE545K, CDKN2A deleted, p53 WT
Pip Tip: The canonical ER+ luminal-A breast model and the standard background for ERα / SERD / ER-PROTAC work (e.g. benchmarking vs fulvestrant). Remember the insulin supplement — ATCC's formulation includes 0.01 mg/mL insulin, and leaving it out slows growth. For hormone-independent assays, switch to phenol-red-free EMEM + charcoal-stripped FBS ≥48 h before treatment. Beware clonal drift — MCF7 stocks from different labs have been shown to differ functionally, so re-validate ER signalling if using an old vial.
🧫 MIA PaCa-2 Pancreatic carcinoma · ATCC CRL-1420 ATCC page
Phase contrast · © ATCC
Organism / tissueHuman · pancreas, adult male; ductal adenocarcinoma
MorphologyEpithelial, adherent — with persistent floating population
Complete mediumDMEM (ATCC 30-2002) + 10% FBS + 2.5% horse serum
Atmosphere37 °C · 95% air / 5% CO2
Doubling time~40 h
Subculture0.25% trypsin-EDTA · split 1:3 to 1:8 · 2×/week
Key featuresKRASG12C, TP53R248W, CDKN2A deleted, SMAD4 WT
Pip Tip: Don't skip the horse serum — ATCC formulates with FBS and 2.5% HS; dropping it changes the phenotype. The line carries KRAS G12C, so it's one of the standard cellular backgrounds for sotorasib/adagrasib benchmarking and for KRAS-targeting PROTAC work. A floating sub-population is normal and genuinely viable — if you want consistent seeding, collect the supernatant, spin gently, and pool with the trypsinised adherent fraction.
🧫 VCaP Prostate carcinoma (vertebral metastasis) · ATCC CRL-2876 ATCC page
Phase contrast · © ATCC
Organism / tissueHuman · prostate adenocarcinoma; vertebral metastasis
MorphologyEpithelial-like; small tightly-packed clusters + floating clusters + debris
Complete mediumDMEM (ATCC 30-2002) + 10% FBS
Atmosphere37 °C · 95% air / 5% CO2
Doubling timeVery slow — ≥2 weeks to ~50% confluence
Subculture0.25% trypsin-EDTA (extended) · split 1:2 to 1:3 · every 1–2 weeks
Key featuresTMPRSS2-ERG fusion +, AR amplified, p53 mutant — reflects clinical mCRPC
Pip Tip — read before thawing: VCaP is genuinely hard work. Expect up to 48 h to attach post-thaw and after each subculture, and ≥2 weeks to reach ~50% confluence with a dense mixture of adherent cells, floating clusters and moderate-to-heavy debris at all times — all of this is normal. Cells recover better in a T-25 than a T-75. Do not discard floating cells at medium changes or subcultures — spin them down gently and add them back to the adherent population. Cells attach as small tightly-formed clusters + some singles, then spread as flattened epithelial-like islands. Despite the pain, it's the line for TMPRSS2-ERG biology and a key AR-amplified mCRPC model for AR / ERG degrader work. (Handling notes paraphrased from the ATCC CRL-2876 product sheet.)
🧫 HAP-1 Human haploid · Horizon Discovery C631 Horizon page
Organism / tissueHuman · near-haploid; derived from KBM7 (CML blast-crisis line)
MorphologyFibroblast-like, adherent (unusual for CML origin)
Complete mediumIMDM + 10% FBS
Atmosphere37 °C · 5% CO2
Doubling time~24 h
Subculture0.25% trypsin-EDTA · split 1:4–1:8 · 3×/week; passage <20
Key featuresNear-haploid genome — single Cas9 cut = complete KO; gold standard for CRISPR screens and target validation
💧 Pip Tip: Haploidy means one cut = one KO — no biallelic editing needed. Watch for spontaneous diploidisation after ~20 passages; keep early frozen stocks and validate ploidy by flow cytometry for long experiments. Sensitive to over-trypsinisation (2–3 min RT). IMDM is essential — DMEM gives slower growth.
🧫 SU-DHL-5 Diffuse large B-cell lymphoma · ATCC CRL-2958 ATCC page
Phase contrast · © ATCC
Organism / tissueHuman · lymph node; diffuse large cell B-cell lymphoma (GCB subtype), 17-year-old female
MorphologyLymphoblast-like, suspension (non-adherent), small round cells in loose clumps
Complete mediumRPMI-1640 (ATCC 30-2001) + 10% heat-inactivated FBS
Atmosphere37 °C · 5% CO2, humidified
Doubling time~40 h
SubcultureMaintain 0.1–0.8 × 106 viable cells/mL · split 1:2–1:5 every 2–3 days; feed 2–3×/week; post-thaw viability may be <30% — normal
Key featuresGCB-DLBCL model; CD19+, CD20+, CD10+; CDKN2A/B homozygous deletion; PTEN altered; del(6q13), +12; TP53 mutant
Pip Tip: The go-to GCB-subtype DLBCL model for lymphoma target degrader work. Suspension growth means no trypsin — just count and dilute. Post-thaw viability routinely drops below 30%; this is documented by ATCC and does not indicate a bad vial. Let it recover at low density (0.5 × 106/mL in a 24-well plate) for 24–48 h before ramping up. Viability at saturation typically plateaus around 80–85%. Key genetic context: GCB phenotype (CD10+), homozygous CDKN2A/p16 loss, and PTEN mutation — relevant if studying CDK4/6, PI3K/AKT, or BCL2/BCL6 degraders in lymphoma.
Lab Kits
Quick-reference for common kits — catalog numbers, storage, key tips
🧬
GeneJET Miniprep
Thermo Fisher · K0502 (50 preps) / K0503 (250 preps)
DNA purification Plasmid
🌡 RT (kit); 4°C (elution buffer)
  • Resuspend pellet fully in RNase A solution before lysis — vortex if needed
  • Elute with 50–100 µL pre-warmed EB (70°C) for maximum yield
  • Expected yield: 1–10 µg from 1.5 mL overnight culture
🔬
NucleoSpin Gel & PCR Cleanup
Macherey-Nagel · 740609 (50) / 740609.250 (250)
PCR cleanup Gel extraction
🌡 RT
  • Gel extraction: solubilise at 50°C, 5–10 min — vortex every 2 min
  • Add EtOH to NT3 buffer if precipitate forms (check insert)
  • Elute with 15–30 µL NE buffer (5 mM Tris-HCl, pH 8.5) for high concentration
🔗
NEBuilder HiFi Assembly
NEB · E2621S (10 rxn) / E2621L (50 rxn)
Gibson assembly Cloning
❄️ −20°C
  • 50°C × 15 min (≤3 fragments) or 60 min (4+ fragments)
  • 15–30 bp overlaps; design with the Gibson calculator above
  • Transform 2 µL into NEB Stable (C3040H) on ice 5–10 min
✂️
BbsI (Type IIS Restriction Enzyme)
NEB · R0593S — 1,000 units
CRISPR cloning PX458 linearisation
❄️ −20°C · stable ≥12 months
  • 37°C × 30 min in CutSmart Buffer — no BSA needed
  • HF version (R3539) available if star activity is a concern
  • Use Digest calculator (PX458 → CRISPR section) for volumes
🦠
NEB Stable Competent E. coli
NEB · C3040H (20 × 50 µL tubes)
Transformation Lentiviral / large plasmids
❄️ −80°C · thaw 5–10 min on ice
  • Use for PX458, lentiviral vectors, large or unstable plasmids
  • Heat shock 42°C × 30 s (not longer)
  • Resuspend in 50 µL SOC/LB and plate all — incubate 30°C overnight
🥚
Pierce BCA Protein Assay
Thermo Fisher · 23225 (500 assays) / 23227 (micro)
Protein quantification
🌡 RT · Reagent A stable indefinitely; Reagent B 1 yr
  • Mix Reagent A:B = 50:1; working reagent stable 1 h at RT
  • Standard protocol: 37°C × 30 min; read 562 nm
  • Compatible with most detergents; avoid >5% SDS or >0.5 M NaOH
💡
CellTiter-Glo 2.0
Promega · G9242 (100 mL) / G9243 (400 mL)
Cell viability Luminescence
❄️ −65°C or 4°C ≤2 months after thaw · avoid >25°C
  • Equilibrate plates and reagent to RT before use
  • Equal volume reagent:media; mix 2 min, incubate 10 min, read
  • Use the CTG2 calculator (Cell Biology section) for volumes
✨
HiBiT Lytic Detection System
Promega · N3040 (100 assays, 96-well)
HiBiT tagged proteins Degradation assay
❄️ −80°C (LgBiT, substrate); 4°C stable ≥6 months
  • LgBiT dilution 1:100 in Detection Buffer; substrate 1:50
  • Mix equal volume lytic reagent:cell media — full lysis in ~5 min
  • Use HiBiT Lytic Calculator (Cell Biology) for full volumes
💉
Lipofectamine RNAiMAX
Thermo Fisher · 13778030 (1.5 mL) / 13778150 (7.5 mL)
siRNA transfection Reverse transfection
4°C · stable ≥12 months
  • Reverse transfection: add complex to empty well, then seed cells on top
  • Dilute both siRNA and reagent in Opti-MEM; combine, incubate 5 min
  • Typical: 1.5 µL reagent + 5 nM siRNA in 96-well; scale with cell number
💊
Lipofectamine 3000
Thermo Fisher · L3000001 (1.5 mL) / L3000015 (7.5 mL)
Plasmid transfection PX458 delivery
4°C · stable ≥12 months
  • Requires P3000 Reagent (included) — do NOT skip; it boosts efficiency
  • Dilute DNA + P3000 separately from Lipofectamine; combine and incubate 15 min
  • Change media 6 h post-transfection to reduce toxicity
Pip Tip
Missing a kit? Let me know and I'll add it. Catalog numbers are current as of 2024 — check supplier websites for updated versions or regional equivalents.
Tools
Lab timers · Experiment planner · PROTAC analytics
Tools Timing · Planning
PROTAC Degradation analytics
Lab Timers
Multiple named countdown timers — run in parallel
New Timer
: :
No timers yet — tap a quick preset or set a custom time above.
The Story of Pip
LabMate's mascot — a brief history
Chapter I · The Beginning
Born in a buffer tube
Pip first appeared one morning in the Ciulli Lab when someone needed to remember that DMSO < 0.1% and couldn't find the protocol. A small droplet drawn on a sticky note — neutral expression, no complaints. That was Pip: calm, informative, never judging.

In the early days, Pip only appeared in the Quick Calculators tab. One tip about dilution factors. One reminder about pH. Nothing more.
Chapter II · The Golden Era
Pip discovers CRISPR
When the CRISPR tab was added, Pip became genuinely excited. gRNA design, electroporation, single-cell sorting — so much to say. The tips multiplied. Pip started adding bold text and bullet points.

The lab noticed. "Pip seems really into CRISPR," someone said. They weren't wrong.
Chapter III · The Dark Times
The great renaming
The app was called Pipette. Then flask. Then bench. Then LABCALC. Each rename was confusing. Pip said nothing, but the stress was visible.

Profile links still encoded as #flask=… long after anyone remembered why. Pip carried the legacy silently.
Chapter IV · The Comeback
LabMate is born
In 2025, the app finally got a name that stuck: LabMate. Pip got proper CSS classes, multiple poses, and a base64-embedded existence that survived OneDrive sync failures.

Pip was smug about this. Justifiably.
Chapter V · Today
Pip Tip, not Pip says
The tips are now called Pip Tips — as it always should have been. Pip covers 40+ protocols across molecular biology, CRISPR, proteomics, biophysics, structural biology, and PROTAC chemistry.

Pip thumbs up. The lab is happy. Science is hard enough without bad UX.
Chapter VI · The Hub Era
Pip moves into a bigger house
A new launcher arrived: The Hub. Suddenly LabMate, Echo, and Degradation Explorer all lived under one roof — a clean grid of cards, a single browser tab, no more hunting through folders.

Echo gained two new assay modes: FP and TR-FRET. The "Dmax" label quietly became "Span" for anything that wasn't a HiBiT degradation — a small change that haunted no one except Pip, who had been personally attached to the word Dmax.

Degradation Explorer grew up too: error bars, configurable quality thresholds, a CSV export. LabMate quietly acquired PK and pH calculators. Four new apps were born — Figure Forge, Plate Designer, Kinetics, Protein Tools — and joined The Hub with fresh logos and matching accents.

Pip was asked to leave the header. "Standardize the style across the Hub," they said. Pip packed a small droplet-shaped bag and retreated here, to this hidden story page, where only those who click the logo five times will find them.

Pip is still here. Just… quieter.
Epilogue
What does Pip want?
Nobody knows. Pip remains confused about this question. Perhaps the real Pip was the protocols we wrote along the way.

You found this page by clicking the logo 5 times. Pip is impressed.
Pip's known expressions
Neutral
Excited
Stressed
Smug
Thumbs Up
Confused
💧 Pip — Ciulli Lab mascot since 2024  ·  Single-file HTML, fully self-contained
flask
Edit structure — Marvin JS
💧 Pip's story unlocked!
Notes
Saved
Pip’s tips
